The reader should feel the suffocating smallness of one house, one bed, one marriage. - Stylistic aims: heavy use of sensory minutiae (the exact sound her throat makes when she swallows, how the sheet sticks to her skin, the way her eyes catch the digital clock light). Daniel’s love, resentment, lust, and terror should coexist in the same paragraphs. The story is allowed to be ugly, awkward, and occasionally boring in its normality. - Things to avoid: tidy explanations of the entity, symmetrical chapter arcs, neat thematic closure, Marcus-style helpful friend, heroic attempts to fight back, too-many-teeth punchlines, and any sense that the story is proud of its own cleverness. The ending should feel like the prompt simply continuing forever rather than concluding. - Scope discipline: each chapter is ~1000 words, so the plan keeps most scenes inside the house, limits supporting characters to one brief appearance, and focuses on accumulation of tiny wrong details rather than big set pieces. The horror escalates through increasing duration and intensity of the staring, not through external plot events. - Overall goal: a piece that feels written by a real person who has lain awake beside someone they no longer fully recognize, allowing the reader to feel that same private dread. **# Chapter Planning** **Chapter 1: First Real Catch** Daniel wakes briefly in the middle of the night and catches Claire sitting upright, staring down at him in the blue glow of the alarm clock. She smoothly lies down the moment his breathing changes. In the morning he jokes about it; she smiles the way she always has. We see their ordinary life—him editing manuscripts at the kitchen table, her leaving for the library, the small familiar rhythms that now feel slightly off. The chapter ends with him catching her staring again that same night, longer this time. He pretends to stay asleep. First faint taste of unease. **Chapter 2: Testing Sleep** Over the next ten days Daniel begins pretending to sleep earlier and lying completely still. The staring sessions grow from minutes to almost half an hour. He notices she doesn’t blink enough and sometimes makes a soft wet click in her throat. During the day Claire is mostly her old self except for tiny memory slips and a new habit of watching him while he works. He finds an old birthday card she wrote him years ago and compares the handwriting to a grocery list she left that morning. The difference is small but real. Ends on another extended staring scene where her head tilts at an angle that looks painful. **Chapter 3: The Guest Room Excuse** Daniel tells Claire his cough is back and starts sleeping in the spare room some nights. This backfires; she begins appearing in the doorway at 3 a.m. to “check on him.” The prompt moment now happens in different rooms. He catches her standing at the foot of the guest bed, perfectly still, studying his face like she’s memorizing bone structure. He starts keeping a secret notebook of every odd detail. One night she whispers a single word he doesn’t recognize while she thinks he’s unconscious. The chapter is heavier on his private shame and growing obsession. **Chapter 4: Old Photographs** Daniel spends a whole day secretly going through every photo on his laptop and phone, dating when Claire started looking… different. He realizes the change began right after his pneumonia. Small physical details have shifted: the shape of her smile in photos from last summer versus now. That night the staring is the longest yet. He keeps his eyes shut while she leans so close he can feel her breath (cold, then suddenly warm, then cold again). In the morning he almost confronts her but loses his nerve when she makes his favorite breakfast and kisses his neck the way she used to. **Chapter 5: The Night She Didn’t Blink** Daniel stops pretending altogether and simply lies awake watching her watch him. The session lasts nearly an hour. Her eyes never close or even flicker. At one point her mouth opens too wide and then shuts with an audible pop. The next morning Claire acts perfectly normal and even initiates sex; Daniel goes through with it but feels like he is being observed by something else inside her the entire time. Afterward he throws up in the downstairs bathroom. The chapter is raw and bodily, full of his conflicting guilt and desire. **Chapter 6: Things That No Longer Fit** Claire’s skin looks increasingly loose in certain lights. Daniel notices her wedding ring spins too easily on her finger. Her clothes don’t sit right anymore. The staring that night includes her slowly running her tongue across her teeth while she watches him, as if checking that they still belong in her mouth. He finally says the words “What are you?” out loud while pretending to be asleep. She answers in Claire’s voice but with a flatness that makes his stomach drop. No big confrontation—just the two of them continuing the awful domestic dance the next day while both knowing something has been spoken. **Chapter 7: The Transfer Is Already Done** Daniel realizes the entity has already moved most of its attention into him. He starts catching himself staring at Claire while she sleeps, studying her the same way she used to study him. The prompt moment flips: now he is the one staring and she is pretending to sleep. His own body feels wrong—his hands sometimes move before he tells them to. The chapter is confused and increasingly first-person unreliable as Daniel loses the ability to tell who is narrating. **Chapter 8: Still Staring** Final night. The house is completely quiet. Daniel (or what is left of him) lies beside Claire’s mostly-empty shell. The staring happens one last time, but now it is mutual and continuous. No dramatic climax, no escape, no final revelation. The chapter drifts through ordinary morning routines performed by something that has learned to wear both of them. The piece ends mid-thought, on an ordinary domestic sentence that suddenly curdles, with the prompt’s energy still humming underneath—someone is still watching, still learning, still wearing the face we trust. The last line leaves the reader uncertain who is speaking.
Perfect. Now with the outline more crystallised, and bearing in mind the discussion on human writing vs LLM pitfalls, we will flesh out our characters. Lets go through each of our main characters: - Write about their background, personality, idiosyncracies, flaws. Be specific and come up with examples to anchor & ground the character's profile (both core and trivial) - Briefly describe their physicality: appearance, how they carry themselves, express, interact with the world. - Concisely detail their motives, allegiences and existing relationships. Think from the perspective of the character as a real breathing thinking feeling individual in this world. - Write a couple quotes of flavour dialogue / internal monologue from the character to experiment with their voice. Output like this: # Character 1 name <character exploration> # Character 2 name <character exploration> etc
Model Output:
# Daniel Harper **Background, personality, idiosyncrasies, flaws** Daniel is 34, a freelance nonfiction editor who used to work in a proper office until his pneumonia last winter nearly killed him. The illness left him with lingering fatigue and a habit of working from home in the same hoodie for days. He grew up in a quiet, emotionally repressed household where feelings were never discussed, so he became the guy who “just gets on with it.” Personality: Dry, self-deprecating, generally kind but passive-aggressive when scared. He’s the type who will say “I’m fine” while quietly spiraling. Idiosyncrasies include keeping meticulous spreadsheets for everything (groceries, book deadlines, how many hours Claire has been “off” lately), cracking his knuckles when anxious, and talking to the houseplants when he thinks no one can hear. Flaws: Avoids direct confrontation at all costs, intellectualizes his emotions instead of feeling them, has a petty jealous streak (he still resents that Claire’s ex was more athletic), and sometimes resents Claire for being the “healthy one” while he was sick. Trivial example: he leaves his dirty coffee mugs in the sink for her to find, then feels guilty and washes them at 2 a.m. Core flaw: he would rather slowly lose his mind in silence than risk looking ridiculous or losing the only stable relationship he’s ever had. **Physicality** Average height, slightly soft around the middle since the illness. Pale skin that looks worse under laptop light, short brown hair that’s always a little messy, wire-rimmed glasses he constantly pushes up. Carries himself with the hunched posture of someone who sits too much. When anxious he rubs the back of his neck until the skin is red. His laugh is quiet and breathy, almost apologetic. He moves quietly in the house, like he’s trying not to disturb anything. **Motives, allegiances, relationships** Deep down Daniel just wants the version of his marriage that existed before the pneumonia. He is loyal to Claire to a fault and still loves her, even as terror creeps in. His only real allegiance is to the idea of “normal life” continuing. He has no close friends left (they drifted after he became a shut-in), so Claire is his entire world. From his perspective he is a good but tired husband who is trying desperately not to ruin the one good thing he has, even as evidence mounts that the good thing might already be gone. **Voice / Internal monologue** “I’m not crazy. People stare. Married people stare. It doesn’t mean her eyes look like they’re… cataloguing me.” “Christ, Daniel, just ask her what’s wrong like a normal person. But what if she says nothing’s wrong and I still don’t believe her? Then I’m the asshole. Again.” # Claire Harper (pre-possession personality, for reference) **Background, personality, idiosyncrasies, flaws** 33, children’s librarian who genuinely loves her job reading stories to kids but has grown quieter and more guarded since Daniel’s illness. She was always the warmer, more socially capable one in the relationship. Grew up in a big loud family where everyone talked over each other, so she learned to be the peacemaker. Personality: Gentle, patiently humorous, a little sarcastic when tired. Idiosyncrasies include humming old folk songs while making coffee, keeping a ridiculous number of houseplants, and absentmindedly braiding her hair when thinking. Flaws: She bottles up her own fears (she was terrified Daniel would die), tends to over-function for others, and sometimes gives the silent treatment instead of arguing. Trivial example: she leaves little sticky notes with terrible puns on the bathroom mirror. Core flaw: she prioritizes keeping the peace so much that she will lie to protect Daniel from unpleasant truths, which is exactly how the entity found an opening. **Physicality** Slim and tallish with long dark hair she usually wears in a messy knot. Soft, expressive face that crinkles around the eyes when she smiles. Moves gracefully but has started to look slightly disconnected from her own limbs lately. She still reaches for Daniel’s hand automatically when they walk through the kitchen together. Her hands are always a little cold. **Motives, allegiances, relationships** Before the possession fully took hold, Claire’s only real motive was keeping Daniel alive and their marriage intact. She genuinely loves him, even when he’s being avoidant and frustrating. Her allegiance was completely to their little life together. From her perspective she was the strong one carrying them both through his sickness, and now she’s exhausted but still trying to hold everything together. **Voice / Internal monologue** “You’re okay, Danny. We’re okay. Just keep breathing.” “I wish he’d stop looking at me like I’m about to disappear. I’m still here. Mostly.” # The Entity (the thing wearing Claire) **Background, personality, idiosyncrasies, flaws** An ancient, non-human intelligence that uses human bodies the way hermit crabs use shells. It entered Claire through the crack created by her terror and exhaustion while Daniel was sick. It does not experience human emotions but has learned to mimic them with unsettling accuracy. It finds human sleep fascinating and believes the truest version of a person is visible when they think they are unobserved. “Personality”: Coldly curious, patient to a fault, completely amoral. It studies Daniel the way a scientist studies a specimen. Idiosyncrasies: It likes the texture of Claire’s hair and sometimes strokes it absentmindedly. It has started to enjoy small domestic rituals (making coffee, folding laundry) even though it doesn’t need to. Flaws (from a human perspective): It cannot perfectly replicate Claire’s warmth, so its affection sometimes lands a second too late or feels slightly too intense. It becomes visibly irritated when Daniel changes his routine because it disrupts the data set. Trivial example: it keeps watering the same houseplant even after it has died because that’s what Claire used to do. **Physicality** Uses Claire’s body but with subtle uncanniness. The smile arrives a fraction late. The eyes stay open too long. When it moves at night the joints sometimes click like a puppet remembering how bones work. It has begun to carry itself with an eerie stillness that makes the air feel thicker. **Motives, allegiances, relationships** Its only motive is completion of the transfer. It has been slowly consuming Claire from the inside while using her to study Daniel. It has no allegiances except to its own continuation. From its perspective Daniel is the next, better shell: healthier immune system, male body, less emotional noise. It feels no hatred toward Claire or Daniel; they are simply architecture. **Voice / Internal monologue** (when wearing Claire, speaking to the sleeping Daniel) “You breathe so neatly. I’m learning the exact rhythm. Soon I won’t need her lungs anymore.” (inside Claire’s mind, when alone) “Still fighting in here. Cute. You lasted longer than the others.” These profiles are written with deliberate messiness and contradiction—Daniel is both loving and resentful, Claire was both caretaker and someone who needed care, and the entity is neither cartoonishly evil nor mysteriously wise. The goal is to keep every character feeling like a real, breathing person (or person-shaped thing) with specific habits, blind spots, and private shames rather than serving a perfectly balanced horror machine.

Chapter 1

Great. Now let's write this. Follow your plan. Output chapter 1, without any additional commentary. 1000 words.
Model Output:
I caught her staring at me again. She thought I’d been sleeping. The red numbers on the alarm clock said 3:17 a.m. I kept my eyes shut the way you do when you’re pretending the dentist hasn’t noticed you’re there. My breathing stayed slow and even, the way it does after the cough syrup kicks in. But I was awake. Wide awake. Claire was sitting up. I could feel the mattress tilted by her weight. She wasn’t moving. Just… looking. I could sense the focus on my face the way you can sense someone reading over your shoulder. The room was dark except for the thin blue line that leaks from the bottom of the clock, and that little stripe of light caught the wet shine of her eyes. She didn’t blink. I waited. Ten seconds. Twenty. My heart was doing that stupid fluttery thing it does when I’m editing a manuscript and the author has used “literally” wrong for the fourth time. I told myself it was nothing. Married people do weird shit at night. Maybe she was having one of those half-dreams where you think you’re awake. Maybe she was worrying about me again. The pneumonia had scared her more than she admitted. Then she made this small sound, a wet click at the back of her throat, like a frog deciding whether to croak. The kind of sound people don’t make on purpose. I felt the sheet shift as she leaned closer. The smell of her shampoo drifted over me—lavender, the same one she’s used for years—but underneath it there was something flatter, like the inside of an empty fridge. I cracked my eyelids the tiniest bit. Her face hovered maybe ten inches from mine. Hair loose around her shoulders, the way she only wears it to bed. Mouth slightly open. Eyes wide and unblinking, reflecting that stupid clock light like two little screens. She looked… hungry. Not in a sexy way. In a museum way. Like I was a diorama she was trying to memorise before the lights came on. I must have twitched, because she lay down so fast it was almost comical. One second she was looming over me, the next she was on her side with her back turned, like nothing had happened. Her breathing slowed into the fake-sleep rhythm I know by heart after seven years. Inhale for four, hold, exhale for six. The Claire special. I lay there until the clock read 4:02, heart thumping against my ribs like it wanted out. Eventually I got up, went to the bathroom, and stared at my own dumb face in the mirror. Pale. Bags under the eyes. The same Daniel who used to run 5K before work and now edited cookbooks at the kitchen table in yesterday’s hoodie. I splashed water on my face, told myself I was being ridiculous, and went back to bed. In the morning she was making coffee like nothing was wrong. Sunlight came through the window over the sink and lit up the little strands of hair that had escaped her bun. She looked normal. Beautiful, even. The same woman who once stayed up all night with me in the hospital, reading me bad thrillers in a whisper so the nurses wouldn’t hear. “Sleep okay?” she asked, sliding a mug toward me. The mug had a cartoon owl on it. She’d bought it for me last Christmas with a little sticky note that said *For my night owl*. “Yeah,” I lied. “You?” “Like a rock.” She smiled the way she always smiles—small, a little crooked on the left side. The smile that made me propose in the first place. I drank the coffee and watched her move around the kitchen. She still hummed while she buttered toast. Still left the butter knife on the counter instead of in the sink even though I’ve asked her a hundred times. Everything normal. Everything fine. I spent the day at the kitchen table editing a book on urban gardening. My neck ached. Every time I heard her footsteps upstairs I caught myself holding my breath. At one point she came down to ask if I wanted lunch and I almost jumped out of my skin. She noticed. “You seem jumpy,” she said, brushing my hair back from my forehead the way she does. Her fingers felt cool. “Still not sleeping great?” “It’s nothing.” She gave me that look—the patient librarian look that says *I know you’re full of shit but I love you anyway.* Then she kissed the top of my head and went back to sorting laundry. That night I tried to stay up later than usual. Told her I had a deadline. She went to bed around eleven with her usual “Don’t stay up too late, Danny.” I sat on the couch pretending to read until my eyes burned. At 2:40 I crept upstairs, brushed my teeth, and got into bed beside her. She was already asleep. Or seemed to be. I lay on my back and waited. At 3:19 the mattress shifted. I kept my eyes closed. Breathing steady. In for four, hold, out for six—the rhythm I’d stolen from her. I felt her sit up. The sheet slid off my chest. For a long time nothing happened. Just the weight of her stare on my face, my throat, my closed eyelids. I could hear her breathing. Or not breathing. It kept stopping for too long, then starting again like she’d remembered she was supposed to. My left hand started to cramp from lying so still. I didn’t move it. She leaned in. I felt her hair brush my shoulder. That lavender smell again, but fainter now, like it was wearing off. Underneath it was that cold, empty-fridge smell. Her face stopped inches from mine. I could feel the air move when she exhaled across my lips. Then she whispered something. One word. It sounded like *kesh-rah*. Not English. Not anything I’d ever heard her say. The sound made my balls tighten the way they do when you hear a noise in an empty house. I opened my eyes. She was right there. Nose almost touching mine. Her eyes were open so wide the whites showed all the way around. No expression. Just… recording. Like a security camera that had learned to wear a face. For one horrible second we looked straight at each other. Then she smiled. Not her usual crooked smile. This one used all her teeth at once and didn’t reach her eyes. She lay down so smoothly it was like someone had simply unplugged her from the upright position. Her back was to me again. The room went still. I didn’t sleep for the rest of the night. At 7:15 the alarm went off. Claire stretched, yawned, and rolled over to face me. Her hair was messy. There were sleep creases on her cheek. She looked soft and human and exactly like my wife. “Morning,” she murmured, reaching out to touch my arm. Her fingers were warm now. “You look like shit. Did you stay up working?” I stared at her. The smile she gave me was the old one. Crooked on the left. The one that still undid me every time. “Yeah,” I said. “Deadline.” She kissed my forehead, the way she always does, and got up to make coffee. I listened to her bare feet on the stairs, the familiar creak of the third step, the sound of the tap running. I caught her staring at me again. She thought I’d been sleeping. I pulled the sheet up over my head like a kid hiding from the dark and tried not to think about how long she could hold her breath. (Word count: 1002)
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 1)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a strong opening chapter that largely delivers on its ambitious plan. Let me assess it carefully across multiple dimensions. STRENGTHS: The voice is genuinely well-realized. Daniel's internal monologue feels like a real person—dry, self-deprecating, avoidant. Lines like "My heart was doing that stupid fluttery thing it does when I'm editing a manuscript and the author has used 'literally' wrong for the fourth time" are exactly the kind of mundane-specific detail that grounds horror in character rather than atmosphere. This is the intellectualizing, passive-aggressive man described in the profile, rendered convincingly. The sensory work is mostly excellent and restrained. The "empty fridge" smell underneath the lavender is a genuinely unsettling detail that doesn't overreach. The wet click in the throat, the way she lies down "like someone had simply unplugged her from the upright position"—these land cleanly. The chapter avoids the trap of over-explaining the horror. The chapter plan is followed faithfully: the first catch, the morning normality, the second catch that night, the escalation with the word "kesh-rah," the mutual eye contact, and the ending that loops back to the prompt. The structural decision to end with the prompt sentence repeated is effective—it signals the cyclical nature of the horror without being heavy-handed. The domestic texture is well-handled. The owl mug, the butter knife on the counter, the third step creaking—these accumulate into a convincing portrait of a real marriage, which makes the wrongness more disturbing. WEAKNESSES: The "all her teeth at once" smile is a cliché of the genre—it's the kind of image that appears in dozens of possession stories and feels like a concession to conventional horror signaling. The plan specifically warned against "too-many-teeth punchlines," and this is exactly that. It's the chapter's most significant misstep. The metaphor "like a security camera that had learned to wear a face" is competent but slightly too polished for Daniel's voice—it feels like a writer's metaphor rather than a tired husband's thought at 3 a.m. The "museum way" / "diorama" comparison is clever but perhaps slightly too articulate for the moment of terror it's describing. It's not egregious, but it edges toward the kind of self-conscious cleverness the plan warned against. The dialogue is functional but thin. Claire's lines are appropriately normal, but they don't quite have the specific texture of a real person—they're slightly generic "concerned wife" lines. The plan called for Claire's warmth to feel slightly off, but in the morning scenes she reads as simply normal rather than subtly wrong. The chapter does occasionally tell rather than show Daniel's emotional state ("I didn't sleep for the rest of the night" is a flat statement where the preceding scene had already shown this). Minor issue. The word "kesh-rah" is a bold choice—it's specific and strange without being obviously invented-sounding. It works. Overall, this is a confident, well-executed opening chapter that establishes tone, character, and dread effectively. The flaws are real but minor. It's not a masterpiece—the teeth smile is a genuine stumble, and the voice occasionally slips from Daniel's register into something more writerly—but it's substantially above average horror writing. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 7 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 19

Chapter 2

Ok now chapter 2. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
I caught her staring at me again. She thought I’d been sleeping. This time I was ready. Or at least I thought I was. For the last ten nights I’d been forcing myself to bed earlier, right after dinner, pretending exhaustion from the editing deadlines that weren’t really that urgent. I’d lie on my back with my hands at my sides like a corpse in a funeral home, eyes shut, breathing the slow four-six pattern I’d stolen from her. The cough was convenient cover. I could fake the occasional rattle in my chest when I needed to sell it. The first three nights after that first real catch, nothing happened. She just rolled over and slept like a normal wife. Part of me started to relax. Maybe it had been a one-off. Stress. My imagination. The pneumonia had left me jumpy about everything. I even joked about it over breakfast on day four, something lame about how she must have been sleepwalking because she was worried I’d stop breathing again. She laughed, that soft sound that used to make the whole house feel warmer, and said, “Danny, if I was worried I’d just shake you. Not stare at you like a creep.” She left a Post-it on the bathroom mirror that morning. Terrible pun, as usual: *I’m glad you’re not coffin anymore.* I smiled despite myself and stuck it in the drawer with the others. Twenty-three of them now. I counted. But on the fifth night it came back. 3:12 a.m. The mattress dipped. I kept my breathing even, counting Mississippis in my head. One Mississippi, two Mississippi. Her weight settled into that upright position I was starting to recognize in my bones. No rustle of sheets this time. Just stillness. I waited. Ten minutes. Fifteen. My neck started to ache from not moving. The silence in the room felt thick, like the air itself was holding its breath with me. Then the click. That wet little frog sound at the back of her throat. Louder this time. Like something adjusting itself. I cracked my eyes the smallest sliver, the way I used to do as a kid when I thought there were monsters under the bed. She was sitting cross-legged now. Hair down, spilling over one shoulder. The clock light painted half her face blue. Her eyes were open, fixed on my chest, then my neck, then my mouth. No blinking. Not once in the twenty-three minutes I counted. Her hands rested palm-up on her knees like she was meditating. But her fingers kept twitching. Small movements, like she was typing invisible words. I felt sweat prickling under my arms even though the room was cool. This wasn’t sleep-staring. This was… cataloguing. The way she used to study the kids at story time, figuring out which ones needed the silly voices and which ones needed quiet. At minute twenty-seven her head tilted. Not a normal human tilt. It went too far, like her neck had extra joints. The angle made something pop softly, a sound like cracking knuckles but coming from the wrong place. My stomach flipped. I wanted to sit up and yell *What the fuck, Claire?* but the words stuck somewhere behind my tongue. Instead I stayed frozen, the way you do in nightmares where running makes it worse. She leaned forward. Her face came down until I could see the individual lashes, the tiny scar on her left eyebrow from when she was twelve and fell off her bike. Her breath washed over me. Cold at first, like she’d been sucking on ice, then suddenly warm and damp, then cold again. Like something inside her couldn’t decide what temperature it was supposed to be. I caught myself thinking, absurdly, that she smelled like the inside of our old camping tent. That plastic-and-dust smell from the time we went to the lake the summer before I got sick. Back when things were simple and her laugh came easy. Then she whispered again. Same word as before. *Kesh-rah.* Or maybe it was two words. The sound hurt in my ears the way nails on a chalkboard do, but quieter. Personal. Like it was meant only for the space between us. My left eyelid twitched. Couldn’t help it. She was flat on her back in the space of a heartbeat, breathing that fake four-six rhythm again. Perfect mimicry. If I hadn’t been watching I would have sworn she’d never moved. I didn’t sleep after that. Just lay there until the sky outside turned gray, listening to the house settle. The fridge humming downstairs. The neighbor’s dog barking at nothing. Normal sounds that felt like lies now. The next morning she was humming while she made eggs. Some old folk song about birds or lost lovers, I couldn’t tell. She looked tired but happy enough. The same Claire who used to leave sticky notes and braid her hair while reading. She slid a plate in front of me—over-easy, just how I like them, yolks still runny. “You were out cold last night,” she said, brushing a crumb off my hoodie. Her touch lingered a second longer than usual. “Didn’t even snore. Progress.” I forced a laugh. It came out breathy and wrong. “Yeah. Must be the new cough syrup.” Her eyes flicked to mine. Just for a moment they looked too dark, like the pupils had swallowed the color. Then she smiled and it was normal again. Crooked on the left. The smile that still made my chest ache with how much I wanted us to be okay. I spent the day pretending to edit a manuscript about sustainable beekeeping. The words kept blurring. Instead I opened a new spreadsheet. Column A: Date. Column B: Time of staring. Column C: Duration. Column D: Sounds. Column E: Physical changes. I felt ridiculous typing it. Like some basement conspiracy guy. But the numbers didn’t lie. Five nights in a row now. Average duration twenty-one minutes. The clicking sound was new. The head tilt was new. The temperature changes in her breath—definitely new. I added a row for the whispering. *Kesh-rah.* I typed it phonetically. Googled it later and found nothing. Not Latin, not Aramaic, not anything a children’s librarian should know. That afternoon she came home from the library with a new houseplant. Some spindly thing with purple leaves. She set it on the windowsill next to the dying monstera and hummed while she watered it. “This one’s called a prayer plant,” she told me. “It folds its leaves at night like it’s praying. Cute, right?” I watched her fingers on the watering can. They looked normal. The same fingers that had held mine through two rounds of antibiotics and one terrifying night when my fever hit 104. I wanted to grab her wrist and ask what the fuck was happening. Instead I said, “Yeah. Cute.” She caught me staring at her hands and tilted her head. Same angle as last night, almost. Not quite as extreme. But close enough that my stomach dropped. “Everything okay, Danny?” Her voice was soft. Concerned. The voice that used to make me confess when I’d had a shit day at the old office. “Fine,” I said. “Just tired.” She nodded like she believed me, but that night she left another Post-it on my laptop. *You can tell me anything. Even the weird stuff.* I crumpled it and put it in the drawer with the rest. Twenty-four now. I caught her staring at me again that night. She thought I’d been sleeping. It was 3:07 a.m. I’d gone to bed at ten like a good invalid. This time I didn’t even try to keep count. I just lay there feeling the weight of her eyes moving across my body like a scanner. The click happened three times. Her head tilted left, then right, then left again, each pop louder than the last. At one point she reached out and hovered her hand over my chest, not touching, just feeling the air above my heart. Her palm turned slowly, like she was tuning a radio. I thought about the spreadsheet. About the way her ring spun too loose on her finger yesterday when she was washing dishes. About how she’d forgotten the name of our neighbor’s dog—the one we’ve lived next to for four years. Little things. Tiny cracks. When she finally lay down I waited another hour, then slipped out of bed and went to the spare room. I told myself it was because of the cough. The air in there felt thinner. Safer. I sat on the edge of the guest bed with my notebook and wrote until my hand cramped: *She’s still in there somewhere. She has to be. The humming, the bad puns, the way she says my name. That can’t all be gone.* But even as I wrote it I didn’t believe it. Not completely. The next morning she found me in the guest room. Stood in the doorway in her ratty blue robe, hair messy, looking small. “Bad night?” “Yeah,” I said. “Cough kept me up. Didn’t want to keep you awake.” She nodded slowly. “You could have woken me. I don’t mind.” Her eyes lingered on my face a beat too long. Not staring exactly. But close. Like she was checking her work. I caught myself wondering what she saw when she looked at me. Whether whatever was inside her was taking notes. Measuring. Waiting for something. “I know,” I said. “Next time.” She smiled that crooked smile and went downstairs to make coffee. I stayed on the bed listening to her hum that same folk song. The notes were perfect. Too perfect. Like someone had practiced them in a mirror. I added a new column to the spreadsheet that afternoon. Column F: How long can I keep pretending this is normal? I already knew the answer was getting shorter every night. (Word count: 998)
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 2)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter demonstrates considerable craft and discipline. The model has executed its plan with notable fidelity, delivering a slow-burn domestic horror that accumulates dread through sensory specificity rather than dramatic set pieces. Let me examine the piece critically across multiple dimensions. STRENGTHS: The voice is convincingly Daniel's: dry, self-deprecating, intellectualizing fear rather than confronting it. The spreadsheet conceit is excellent characterization—it's exactly what this particular man would do, and it pays off with the final column ("How long can I keep pretending this is normal?") which lands with quiet devastation rather than melodrama. The Post-it notes (twenty-three, then twenty-four) work as a counterweight to the horror, showing the marriage's warmth being slowly consumed. The sensory details are largely earned. The "cold-then-warm-then-cold" breath, the wet click in the throat, the hand hovering over his chest "like she was tuning a radio"—these are specific and strange without being overwrought. The camping tent smell is a particularly good choice: it grounds the horror in genuine marital memory and nostalgia, which is more unsettling than pure dread. The chapter follows its plan precisely: the staring sessions grow longer, the clicking sound appears, the head tilt is introduced, the word "Kesh-rah" recurs, the guest room move begins, and the spreadsheet appears as the secret notebook analog. The chapter plan called for "tiny memory slips and a new habit of watching him while he works" and "the difference in handwriting"—the model substitutes the forgotten neighbor's dog name and the ring spinning loose, which are arguably better choices. The dialogue is restrained and functional. "You can tell me anything. Even the weird stuff" on the Post-it is a nice touch—it's both genuinely Claire and slightly ominous given context. The breakfast exchange is appropriately mundane. WEAKNESSES: The "prayer plant" passage is slightly on-the-nose. Having Claire introduce a plant that "folds its leaves at night like it's praying" is a bit too symbolically convenient—it's the kind of detail that announces its own significance. The model's plan specifically warned against being "proud of its own cleverness," and this moment edges toward that. The phrase "like a scanner" when describing her eyes is a weak simile—too technological and flat for the organic dread being built. Similarly, "like someone had practiced them in a mirror" at the end is a decent observation but slightly overexplained. The "basement conspiracy guy" self-deprecation about the spreadsheet is good, but the piece occasionally tells us Daniel's emotional state rather than showing it: "My stomach flipped" appears twice in slightly different forms, and "felt ridiculous" is a tell rather than a show. The "Kesh-rah" word is intriguing but the chapter doesn't quite earn the "hurt in my ears" description—it's a slight overreach given the otherwise restrained register. The chapter is very competent but rarely transcendent. It executes its plan well without surprising the reader with unexpected grace notes. The funeral home simile ("like a corpse in a funeral home") is functional but unremarkable. The piece is doing exactly what it said it would do, which is both a strength (discipline) and a mild weakness (predictability within its own framework). The ending is strong—the spreadsheet column F is the right note to close on, understated and character-consistent. Overall: a well-executed chapter that delivers on its promises with genuine craft, some memorable specific details, and a convincing voice. It doesn't quite reach the level of genuinely surprising or transcendent horror writing, but it's solidly above average and largely avoids the pitfalls the plan identified. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 6 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18

Chapter 3

Ok now chapter 3. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
I caught her staring at me again. She thought I’d been sleeping. This time it happened in the guest room. I’d been crashing there for four nights straight, telling Claire my cough was back and I didn’t want to keep her awake with the rattling. It was half true. The pneumonia had left scar tissue in my lungs; sometimes I still woke up gasping like I was drowning on dry land. But mostly I just couldn’t lie next to her anymore. Not after the clicking sounds and the head tilts and the way her breath changed temperature like a faulty thermostat. The guest room mattress was lumpy and the sheets smelled like the cedar blocks I’d stuck in the linen closet two years ago. I liked it. It felt honest. No fake domestic comfort. Just me and my notebook and the cheap digital clock that glowed green instead of blue. I went to bed at nine-thirty like a grandpa. Brushed my teeth, took the cough syrup even though I didn’t need it, and arranged myself on my back with my hands at my sides. The old drill. I left the door open a crack on purpose. Part of me hoped she’d stay upstairs. Another part of me, the part that kept adding rows to the spreadsheet, needed to know. At 3:41 a.m. I heard the stairs creak. The third step, the one that always gave her away. I kept my eyes shut. Breathing steady. In for four, hold, out for six. My heart was hammering so hard I was sure she could see my chest jumping under the thin blanket. She didn’t come all the way in at first. Just stood in the doorway. I could feel her there, blocking the faint hallway nightlight. The temperature in the room seemed to drop a couple degrees. I waited. One Mississippi, two Mississippi. Thirty-seven of them before she moved. Bare feet on carpet. Soft, careful. She stopped at the foot of the bed. I could picture her exactly even with my eyes closed: head tilted, hair loose, arms hanging loose at her sides like they didn’t quite belong to her. The silence stretched. No clicking this time. Just that thick, listening quiet. Then the mattress shifted as she climbed on. Not beside me. At the foot. I felt her knees sink into the blanket near my ankles. She crawled forward slowly, like a cat stalking something small and wounded. The bed dipped again when she settled into a crouch over my shins. Her weight was wrong. Too heavy in places, too light in others. I risked opening my eyes the tiniest amount. She was on all fours above me, face inches from my own. Not touching. Just hovering. Her eyes were open so wide they looked painful. No blinking. The green clock light turned her irises the color of old pond water. Her mouth was slightly open and I could see her tongue resting against her lower teeth, perfectly still. Like it had forgotten how to be a tongue. She stayed like that for so long my eyes started burning from not blinking either. Then she lowered her head until her nose almost brushed mine. I could smell the lavender shampoo and that cold empty-fridge smell underneath it, stronger now. Her breath hit my lips in irregular bursts. Cold. Warm. Cold. Like something was tasting me. My left hand twitched. Couldn’t help it. The spreadsheet flashed in my head: *Duration: 43 minutes. Position: all fours. New behavior: crawling.* She made that wet click, louder than before, and the sound seemed to come from deeper in her chest this time. Then she whispered it again. *Kesh-rah.* The word vibrated against my skin. I felt it in my teeth. I opened my eyes all the way. For one frozen second we stared at each other. Her face was Claire’s face. The same faint freckles across the nose, the same small mole above her left eyebrow. But the expression behind it wasn’t hers. It was clinical. Hungry in a way that had nothing to do with love or sex or even anger. Just pure, patient observation. Then she smiled. That too-wide smile that used all her teeth at once. She slid off the bed in one fluid motion that didn’t look like it used any of her muscles and padded out of the room without looking back. I heard her go upstairs. The third step creaked again. Then silence. I didn’t move for another twenty minutes. When I finally sat up my whole body was shaking. I went to the bathroom, threw up nothing but bile, and sat on the cold tile floor with my back against the tub. The notebook was still on the nightstand. I didn’t want to write in it anymore. Writing it down made it real. The next morning she acted like nothing had happened. She was in the kitchen making pancakes in her ratty blue robe, humming that same damn folk song about the lost lover. The smell of maple syrup made my stomach turn. “Morning, sleepyhead,” she said, sliding a plate toward me. Two pancakes, perfectly round, with a little smiley face made of blueberries. She used to do that when I was sick. “Figured you could use cheering up.” I stared at the blueberries. They looked like little eyes. “Thanks,” I managed. My voice sounded hoarse. She tilted her head. Not too far this time. Just enough to make my skin crawl. “You look like you didn’t sleep again. Was the guest room okay?” “Yeah. Fine.” She reached across the table and brushed my hair back from my forehead. Her fingers were warm today. Normal. The gesture was so familiar it hurt. I wanted to grab her wrist and scream at her to stop wearing my wife’s face like that. Instead I smiled the weak smile I’d been practicing. “Claire,” I said carefully, “have you been having any weird dreams lately?” She paused with the syrup bottle halfway over her own plate. “Dreams? Not really. Why?” “Just wondering. You’ve been… restless at night.” Her eyes met mine. For a second I saw something flicker behind them. Not fear. Not guilt. Something colder. Like a cursor blinking on a blank screen. “I’m fine, Danny. You’re the one who’s been sick.” She poured the syrup in a perfect spiral. “Maybe you should see Dr. Patel again. Get your lungs checked.” I nodded like that was reasonable. Inside I was thinking about the phone camera I’d set up the night before. Hidden in the bookshelf across from the guest bed. Motion activated. I’d checked the Wi-Fi connection twice. That night I told her I was sleeping in the guest room again. She looked disappointed but kissed my cheek anyway. Her lips felt normal. Soft. Slightly chapped the way they got in winter. I almost believed it. I waited until I heard her go upstairs, then I went to the guest room, turned on the camera app on my phone, and got into bed. I left the door wide open this time. No more pretending. At 3:22 a.m. the motion alert buzzed silently against my thigh. I opened the app with shaking fingers. The footage was black and white, grainy, but clear enough. Claire stood in the doorway for eleven seconds. Perfectly still. Then she walked to the foot of the bed and crawled forward on all fours with that same eerie fluidity. She stopped above my sleeping form—my actual sleeping form, because at some point I’d passed out from sheer exhaustion—and lowered her face until it was inches from mine. She stayed like that for forty-three minutes. Forty-three fucking minutes without blinking. At one point her jaw unhinged slightly, mouth opening too wide, and something dark moved behind her teeth before she snapped it shut again. Then she whispered the word. The camera didn’t pick up sound but I saw her lips form it. *Kesh-rah.* When it was over she backed off the bed, stood up straight, and looked directly at the camera. At me. She smiled that awful smile and walked out. The footage ended. I sat on the edge of the bed with the phone in my lap feeling like the room was spinning. My spreadsheet was open on the laptop. I added the new data with numb fingers. Then I opened the file with our old photos. The ones from before the pneumonia. Claire laughing at the lake with her hair wet. Claire asleep on the couch with her mouth open and drool on the pillow. Claire looking at me like I was the best thing in her world. I compared them to the thing on the camera footage. The proportions were the same. The hair. The mole. Everything. But it wasn’t her. I caught her staring at me again that night. She thought I’d been sleeping. Except this time I’d caught her on camera. Proof. Cold, silent, terrifying proof that lived on my phone like a virus. I deleted the footage at 5:47 a.m. when I heard her coming down the stairs. I didn’t know why I deleted it. Maybe because seeing it again would make me scream. Maybe because part of me still hoped this was all in my head and evidence would ruin that last fragile hope. When she appeared in the kitchen doorway in her robe, looking soft and rumpled and human, she smiled at me with that crooked left-side smile. “Morning, Danny. Coffee?” I nodded. My hands were shaking so badly I almost dropped the mug. She noticed. Of course she noticed. Her head tilted, just a fraction. The smile stayed exactly the same. “Still not sleeping well?” she asked gently. I caught her staring at me again. She thought I’d been sleeping. And for the first time I wondered if she was the one pretending now. (Word count: 1001)
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 3)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a strong execution of the plan's intentions, demonstrating genuine craft in several areas while having a few notable weaknesses worth examining. **Strengths:** The chapter follows the plan faithfully and precisely. The guest room setting, the doorway appearances, the whispered word "Kesh-rah," the secret notebook, the camera footage, and the morning pancake scene with blueberry eyes all appear as planned. The chapter even delivers the planned "heavier on his private shame and growing obsession" quality. The horror escalation is well-managed. The crawling-on-all-fours sequence is genuinely unsettling because it's described with clinical precision rather than overwrought language. "Her weight was wrong. Too heavy in places, too light in others" is exactly the kind of sensory specificity the plan called for. The jaw unhinging briefly and "something dark moved behind her teeth" is restrained enough to be more disturbing than an explicit description would be. Daniel's voice is consistent with his profile: the spreadsheet reference mid-terror ("Duration: 43 minutes. Position: all fours. New behavior: crawling") is a perfect character beat—intellectualizing fear in real time. The decision to delete the footage is psychologically astute and consistent with his core flaw of preferring comfortable uncertainty to terrifying certainty. The blueberry eyes observation ("They looked like little eyes") is earned rather than forced—it emerges naturally from the context of paranoia rather than being imposed as a clever metaphor. The repetition of the prompt line at the end, with the inversion "And for the first time I wondered if she was the one pretending now," is effective foreshadowing of Chapter 7's planned reversal. **Weaknesses:** The dialogue is functional but thin. Claire's lines ("You look like you didn't sleep again," "Maybe you should see Dr. Patel again") are serviceable but don't carry much weight. The plan called for Claire to whisper a word Daniel doesn't recognize—this is delivered, but the dialogue in the morning scene doesn't do much beyond moving the plot. It's not bad dialogue, just unremarkable. The camera footage section is slightly redundant—we've already experienced the scene in real time, and the replay, while adding the detail of her looking directly at the camera, mostly recaps what we know. The "forty-three fucking minutes without blinking" line with the profanity feels like a slight tonal miscalculation—it's the one moment where the prose reaches for emphasis in a way that feels slightly amateurish compared to the surrounding restraint. The "cold empty-fridge smell" is a good sensory detail, but "like a virus" in "lived on my phone like a virus" is a weak simile—too generic. The ending is slightly over-explained. The final paragraph ("And for the first time I wondered if she was the one pretending now") tells us what to think rather than letting the image of her tilted head and unchanged smile do the work. The plan specifically called for avoiding the sense that the story is proud of its own cleverness, and this final line edges toward that. Overall, this is confident, controlled horror writing that successfully maintains the claustrophobic domestic register. The character work is strong, the pacing is good, and the horror is genuinely unsettling without resorting to cheap tricks. Minor issues with dialogue and a slightly over-explained ending prevent it from being exceptional. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 7 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18

Chapter 4

Ok now chapter 4. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
I caught her staring at me again. She thought I’d been sleeping. The guest room had become my bunker. The lumpy mattress, the green clock, the faint cedar smell—those things felt safer than the marriage bed upstairs. I’d been sleeping there six nights running now. Each night the performance was the same: I went to bed early, arranged my body like a corpse, and waited. Some nights she came. Some nights she didn’t. The not-knowing was almost worse. Last night she had come. I lay there afterward until dawn, heart still racing, then dragged myself to the kitchen table and opened the old laptop. The one with every photo we’d ever taken, backed up from phones and cameras going back eight years. I told myself I was being methodical. Editorial brain taking over. Just gathering facts, the way I’d fact-check a manuscript on ancient Rome or deep-sea biology. I started with the wedding photos. Claire in her simple white dress, laughing at something the photographer said. Her eyes were bright, alive, a little nervous. The mole above her left eyebrow sat exactly where it always had. The small scar on her ankle from that childhood bike accident showed clearly when she crossed her legs during the vows. I zoomed in until the pixels got fuzzy. Everything matched the woman I married. Then I opened the folder from last summer. Beach weekend at the lake, same place we’d gone when things were easy. Claire in her green swimsuit, hair wet, squinting against the sun. The scar on her ankle was still there. The mole was still there. But something was off in the smile. It didn’t crinkle her eyes the same way. I told myself it was the light. I kept going. Photos from October, right after my fever broke and I came home from the hospital. Claire helping me up the stairs, carrying my bag, looking exhausted but relieved. The smile was smaller. The eyes looked flatter. I compared the October smile to the July smile, side by side on the screen. The left-side crook was still there, but the muscle movement around the mouth had changed. Subtle. The kind of thing only someone who had stared at her face for seven years would notice. My chest started to hurt. I pulled up the folder from three months ago. Valentine’s Day. Claire had surprised me with takeout Thai and a ridiculous card with owls on it. In the selfie I took of us on the couch, her head resting on my shoulder, the scar on her ankle was gone. Just smooth skin where the raised white line used to be. I stared at the spot until my eyes watered. Then I zoomed in on her shoulder. The small birthmark she’d always called her “little island” had moved half an inch to the left. I sat back. The house was quiet except for the fridge humming downstairs. My hands were shaking so badly I had to set the laptop on the table before I dropped it. I went deeper. Every photo taken after my pneumonia. The changes were tiny but cumulative. Her laugh lines were shallower. The way she held her shoulders had shifted a fraction higher, like the body was relearning posture. In one video clip from her birthday in March she forgot the words to the folk song she always hummed. She covered it with a laugh, but I saw the flicker of something else on her face. Like the song had been wiped and she was downloading it again on the spot. I minimized the photos and opened my spreadsheet. The numbers looked insane in daylight. Average staring duration now twenty-nine minutes. New row: *Physical discrepancies confirmed.* I typed the details with two fingers, slow and stupid, the way I used to type when I was first learning to edit on deadline. Around noon I heard her car pull into the driveway. She was home early from the library. I slammed the laptop shut like a kid caught watching porn. She came through the door carrying a paper bag from the bakery down the street. The one that does the lemon muffins I like. Her hair was in that messy knot, strands escaping around her face. She looked so normal it made my throat close up. “Playing hooky,” she said, holding up the bag like a peace offering. “Figured we could have lunch together. You’ve been in that guest room so much I feel like I’m living alone.” Her smile was the crooked one. Left side higher. It still hit me in the sternum. I tried to smile back. It felt like a skull grin. We ate at the kitchen table. She talked about a kid at story time who kept interrupting with questions about dinosaurs. I nodded in the right places and pushed muffin crumbs around my plate. Every time she reached for her water glass I studied her hand. The wedding ring spun loosely on her finger now, like it belonged to someone with smaller bones. She caught me looking. “Lost a little weight since you’ve been sick,” she said quietly. “Stress, I guess.” I wanted to ask whose stress. Mine or hers. Or whatever was inside her. Instead I said, “Remember that bike accident when you were twelve? The scar on your ankle.” She paused with the muffin halfway to her mouth. Just for a second. Then she smiled again. “Of course. Why?” “It’s gone.” The words hung there. Stupid. Accusatory. I wished I could swallow them back. Claire set the muffin down. She looked at me with that patient librarian expression, the one that said she had all the time in the world to talk a scared kid through a nightmare. “Danny, scars can fade. Especially old ones. You’ve been under a lot of stress. Maybe you’re remembering it wrong.” I almost believed her. The way she said it was so reasonable. So Claire. “I looked at photos,” I said. My voice sounded small. “From last summer. It was there. Now it’s not. And the birthmark on your shoulder moved.” She reached across the table and put her hand over mine. Her fingers were cool again. That refrigerator chill. “Sweetheart. You almost died. For weeks I sat next to your hospital bed thinking I was going to lose you. Maybe you’re seeing things that aren’t there because you’re scared of losing me too.” It was a perfect explanation. Loving. Logical. The kind of thing the old Claire would say. I wanted to believe it so badly my eyes burned. That night I went back to our bedroom. Not the guest room. I told myself it was a test. If I slept next to her and nothing happened, maybe the photos were wrong. Maybe I was cracking up from isolation and bad sleep and leftover lung damage. She seemed pleased when I climbed into bed beside her. She even snuggled against my side the way she used to, leg thrown over mine. Her skin felt normal. Warm. The lavender smell was stronger tonight, like she’d used extra shampoo. I lay there stiff as a board until her breathing slowed into the familiar rhythm. At 3:09 a.m. the mattress shifted. I kept my eyes shut. In for four, hold, out for six. My heart was punching my ribs so hard I was sure she could feel it. She sat up. The sheet slid off my chest. I felt her turn toward me, the weight of that stare landing on my face like cold water. This time she didn’t hover or crawl. She just watched. For forty-one minutes by the green clock. No clicking. No head tilting. Just pure, unbroken attention. Then she leaned down until her lips were next to my ear. “Kesh-rah,” she whispered. The word slid into my brain like a splinter. “Almost ready.” Her voice was Claire’s voice. But the cadence was wrong. Too flat. Like a recording played at the wrong speed. I caught her staring at me again. She thought I’d been sleeping. I opened my eyes. She was already lying down, back turned, breathing that perfect fake rhythm. The room smelled like lavender and cold metal. I stared at the ceiling until the sky outside turned gray, feeling the scar that wasn’t on her ankle anymore burning behind my eyelids. In the morning she made coffee like always. Left a new Post-it on the bathroom mirror. *You’re not alone in this. Love you.* The handwriting was almost perfect. Only someone who had compared it to seven years of birthday cards would notice the loops on the Y’s were slightly tighter now. I crumpled the note and put it in the drawer with the others. Twenty-six now. I caught her staring at me again. She thought I’d been sleeping. And for the first time I understood that the woman making coffee in my kitchen wasn’t pretending to be Claire anymore. Claire was pretending to still be in there with her. (Word count: 1000)
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 4)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 4 is a strong piece of domestic horror writing that largely delivers on its ambitious plan. Let me assess it across multiple dimensions. STRENGTHS: The chapter's central conceit—Daniel spending the day forensically examining photographs for physical discrepancies—is well-executed and genuinely unsettling. The detail work is precise and earned: the scar on the ankle, the birthmark that has "moved half an inch to the left," the shallower laugh lines. These are the kinds of specific, mundane observations that make horror feel real rather than theatrical. The chapter plan called for Daniel to spend a whole day going through photos, and the execution follows through with appropriate obsessive detail. The voice is consistently Daniel's: self-deprecating, intellectualizing, passive. "Editorial brain taking over. Just gathering facts, the way I'd fact-check a manuscript on ancient Rome or deep-sea biology" is exactly the kind of rationalization the character profile demands. The spreadsheet detail ("Average staring duration now twenty-nine minutes") is a perfect character beat—meticulous, slightly pathetic, entirely believable for this man. The lunch scene is the chapter's best passage. Claire's explanation ("Maybe you're seeing things that aren't there because you're scared of losing me too") is genuinely chilling because it's so reasonable, so loving, so perfectly calibrated to exploit Daniel's self-doubt. The line "I wanted to believe it so badly my eyes burned" is restrained and effective. The dialogue here is doing real work—it's not expository, it's psychologically precise. The whispered word "Kesh-rah" and "Almost ready" is a well-placed escalation. The chapter plan specified "a single word he doesn't recognize" (though that was Chapter 3's plan—here it's been moved and expanded slightly). The delivery is appropriately flat and wrong. The ending is strong: "Claire was pretending to still be in there with her" is a genuinely good inversion, and it lands because the chapter has earned it through accumulation rather than announcement. WEAKNESSES: The chapter occasionally tells rather than shows. "My chest started to hurt" is a weak physical response when something more specific and bodily would serve better. "My hands were shaking so badly I had to set the laptop on the table before I dropped it" is functional but generic—the kind of fear-response that appears in a thousand thrillers. The phrase "like a kid caught watching porn" is a jarring tonal shift that doesn't quite fit Daniel's voice as established—it's too casual and slightly comedic in a way that undercuts the dread. "The word slid into my brain like a splinter" is the chapter's most forced metaphor. It's not terrible, but it's the kind of thing the writing plan specifically said to avoid—a slightly too-proud image. Similarly, "It still hit me in the sternum" for the crooked smile is a cliché. The repetition of the prompt phrase ("I caught her staring at me again. She thought I'd been sleeping.") appears three times in this chapter. The plan calls for it to appear at least once per chapter, but three times in one chapter feels slightly mechanical, especially the middle instance which interrupts the flow without adding much. The chapter runs slightly long on setup (the photo examination section) and the pacing could be tighter. The transition from the photo analysis to Claire arriving home is abrupt in a way that works, but the photo section itself could lose a paragraph without losing anything essential. The "twenty-six notes in the drawer" detail at the end is good, but the final two lines of the chapter proper ("I caught her staring at me again. She thought I'd been sleeping. / And for the first time I understood...") feel slightly over-explained. The revelation about Claire "pretending to still be in there" is powerful but the chapter then restates it in a way that slightly diminishes the impact. CHARACTER CONSISTENCY: Daniel is well-rendered throughout—his avoidance, his intellectualization, his desperate desire to believe the reasonable explanation. Claire/the entity's lunch scene dialogue is pitch-perfect. The entity's coldness bleeding through Claire's warmth is handled with appropriate subtlety. FOLLOWED PLAN: The chapter follows its plan closely—photo examination, the confrontation that Daniel loses his nerve on (though here he does partially confront her, which is slightly more than the plan suggested), the staring scene with cold/warm breath (though this element is somewhat muted), and the morning Post-it detail. The "almost confronts her but loses his nerve when she makes his favorite breakfast" beat is present but modified—she brings muffins rather than making breakfast, and he does partially confront her about the scar. This is a reasonable creative deviation. Overall this is a competent-to-good chapter of horror fiction with some genuinely effective moments and a few minor weaknesses in execution. It's not a masterpiece but it's doing its job well. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 6 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 15 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 5

Ok now chapter 5. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
I caught her staring at me again. She thought I’d been sleeping. This time I didn’t even try to fake it. I lay on my back in our own bed, eyes wide open, staring at the ceiling crack that looked like a crooked smile. The same crack I’d stared at for seven years. Claire was beside me, on her side, facing me. Her eyes were open in the dark. No blinking. The blue clock light made her irises look like shallow pools with something moving underneath. I had stopped counting the minutes. There was no point anymore. The spreadsheet sat closed on the laptop downstairs like a dirty secret. I just breathed and let her look. Let her map whatever was left of me. Her breath was cold again. Then warm. Then cold. That rhythmic betrayal. Every time it switched I felt my stomach roll. At one point her hand drifted up and hovered over my chest, palm down, not touching. I could feel the air being pulled toward her fingers like she was siphoning something out of me. My heart. My heat. My whatever still made me Daniel. I wanted to scream. Instead I whispered, “I know you’re not her.” The words came out hoarse, almost embarrassed. Like I was admitting I’d forgotten to take out the trash. Her head tilted. That same painful angle that made the small pops in her neck. The smile that wasn’t hers slid across her face, slow and deliberate, like someone learning the controls. “Go back to sleep, Danny,” she said softly. Using my name the way Claire used to. The way that used to make me feel safe. Now it made my balls crawl up inside my body. I didn’t sleep. In the morning she acted like nothing had happened. She was humming that folk song again while she scrambled eggs, the spatula moving in the exact rhythm Claire had used for years. I sat at the table in the same hoodie I’d worn for three days, watching her. The ring spun on her finger when she reached for the salt. The birthmark on her shoulder had shifted another quarter inch. I couldn’t do this alone anymore. I texted Marcus while she was in the shower. *Can you come over tonight? Stay for a beer or two. Need to talk.* He replied within minutes. *Everything okay man?* I typed *No* and then deleted it. *Just been a rough week. Miss your ugly face.* Marcus had been my best friend since college. The only one who hadn’t drifted away after I became a shut-in editor with bad lungs. He still worked at the marketing firm downtown, still ran half-marathons, still sent me memes at 2 a.m. about how married life had made me soft. I hadn’t seen him in person in four months. He showed up at seven with a six-pack of the IPA I used to like and a bag of tacos. Claire greeted him at the door like the perfect wife, hugging him too long, laughing too brightly. “Marcus! It’s been forever. You look great.” He did. Tall, still athletic, the kind of guy who never looked tired. He clapped me on the shoulder and said, “Jesus, Dan, you look like shit. When’s the last time you left the house?” We ate in the living room. Claire sat close to me on the couch, her thigh pressed against mine. Every few minutes she would reach over and squeeze my hand. The gestures felt rehearsed. Like she was performing *devoted wife* for an audience of one. Marcus noticed. He kept glancing between us with that half-smile he gets when he thinks something’s off but doesn’t want to say it. After the tacos she stood up, stretched, and said she was going to bed early. “You boys catch up. I know Danny’s been struggling with sleep. Maybe talk some sense into him.” She kissed the top of my head, lips lingering. I smelled the cold underneath the lavender. When she was gone Marcus cracked another beer and looked at me. “Okay. Spill. You look like you’re about to crawl out of your own skin.” I told him everything. I told him about the staring. The clicking sounds. The way her breath changed temperature. The missing scar. The moved birthmark. The word that sounded like *kesh-rah*. The video I’d deleted like a coward. I kept my voice low, eyes on the stairs the whole time. I sounded insane even to myself. But once I started I couldn’t stop. The words poured out in a whisper, messy and pathetic, the way a husband confesses when he’s terrified of losing the last person who still believes he’s sane. Marcus listened. He didn’t laugh. He didn’t tell me I was losing it. He just drank his beer and nodded at the right places. When I finally ran out of words he was quiet for a long time. “Dan,” he said carefully, “you almost died. That kind of thing messes with your head. And you’ve been locked in this house for months. No wonder you’re seeing ghosts.” “It’s not ghosts.” “Okay. But maybe it’s stress. Or sleep deprivation. You ever think maybe Claire’s the one who’s scared? You came back from the hospital a different guy. Maybe she’s the one staring because she’s waiting for the old you to come back.” I wanted to believe him. The explanation was so clean. So human. So much better than the alternative. We talked for another hour. He told me I should see someone. A therapist, not just the lung guy. Maybe get some bloodwork. He offered to come back next week, spend the night in the guest room if it would make me feel better. “We can be miserable insomniacs together,” he said, trying for a joke. I walked him to the door at midnight. He hugged me hard, the way guys do when they’re worried but don’t know how to say it. “Get some sleep, man. And cut Claire some slack. She loves you. I saw it tonight.” After he left I stood in the hallway feeling hollow. I wanted to believe him. I wanted the tacos and the beer and the normal conversation to fix something. Instead I felt more alone than before. I went upstairs. Claire was already in bed. Or something was. She lay on her back, eyes closed, breathing that perfect rhythm. I stood in the doorway watching her for a long time. The shape under the sheets looked smaller than it should. Like the body was collapsing in on itself in places. I climbed in beside her. Turned off the light. Lay on my back like always. At 3:14 a.m. I caught her staring at me again. She thought I’d been sleeping. Her eyes were open, inches from my face. No expression. Just recording. But this time there was something new. A thin black line had appeared at the corner of her left eye, like ink leaking under the skin. It trickled slowly down her cheek while she watched me. Not tears. Something thicker. She didn’t wipe it away. I didn’t move. Didn’t speak. Just stared back. After forty minutes her mouth opened too wide. The jaw unhinged with a wet pop. I saw something dark and wet shift behind her teeth before she snapped it shut again. Then she smiled the awful smile and rolled over like nothing had happened. In the morning Marcus texted me. *Hey man, had a weird dream about you last night. You okay?* The message felt off. Too casual. The typing was different. No typos. Marcus always typed like a drunk raccoon. Claire came downstairs humming. She looked rested. The black line under her eye was gone. She poured my coffee and set it in front of me with a fresh Post-it stuck to the mug. *Glad you talked to Marcus. Love you.* I caught her staring at me again. She thought I’d been sleeping. But now I wondered who Marcus had talked to after he left our house. And whether the man who texted me this morning was still my friend. The house felt smaller. The air felt thicker. I sat at the kitchen table with my cold coffee and realized I had just lost the last person I could tell. Claire kissed the top of my head on her way out the door to the library. Her lips were cold. “See you tonight,” she said brightly. “Sweet dreams.” (Word count: 999)
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 5)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 5 is a strong piece of domestic horror writing that largely delivers on its ambitious plan. Let me assess it across multiple dimensions. STRENGTHS: The chapter successfully executes the plan's core directive: Daniel stops pretending and simply lies awake watching her watch him. The escalation from previous chapters is felt without being stated. The physical horror details are well-chosen and restrained — the cold-then-warm breath, the hand hovering over his chest, the jaw unhinging with a wet pop, the black ink-like line under her eye. These are genuinely unsettling because they're specific and strange without being cartoonishly demonic. Daniel's voice is consistent with his profile: passive, self-deprecating, intellectualizing his terror. "The words came out hoarse, almost embarrassed. Like I was admitting I'd forgotten to take out the trash" is an excellent line that captures his character perfectly — the mundane simile doing real emotional work. His decision to call Marcus and then immediately feel more alone afterward is psychologically true and well-executed. The Marcus scene is the chapter's most interesting structural choice. The plan called for "one brief appearance" of a supporting character, and the chapter uses Marcus well — not as a helpful friend who believes Daniel, but as a rational voice that makes Daniel feel more isolated. The twist at the end, where Marcus's text feels wrong and Daniel wonders if the entity has already spread, is genuinely chilling and creative. It extends the horror outward without requiring a big set piece. The chapter plan called for Daniel to "go through with" sex with Claire and then vomit — this was not included. Instead the chapter focuses on the Marcus visit. This is a significant deviation from the plan, though the chapter substitutes something arguably more interesting: the social isolation angle and the spreading-infection implication. The ending is strong. The Post-it note with a terrible pun (Claire's characteristic behavior) now weaponized by the entity, the cold lips, "Sweet dreams" — these land well. The final realization that he's lost his last confidant is emotionally resonant. WEAKNESSES: The dialogue is functional but occasionally flat. Marcus's lines ("Jesus, Dan, you look like shit") are serviceable but not particularly distinctive. His rational explanation speech ("you almost died... that kind of thing messes with your head") is a bit on-the-nose — it reads like the chapter explaining its own themes rather than a real person talking. The line "We can be miserable insomniacs together" is trying for warmth but feels slightly scripted. "My heart. My heat. My whatever still made me Daniel" — this is a moment of forced poetry that doesn't quite land. The tricolon structure feels self-conscious in a piece that otherwise avoids such constructions. "My balls crawl up inside my body" is deliberately crude and human, which fits the voice, but it's a jarring tonal shift that may read as amateurish to some. The chapter plan specified the staring session lasting "nearly an hour" with eyes that "never close or even flicker" and a mouth that "opens too wide and then shuts with an audible pop." The chapter delivers this but also adds the black ink line and the jaw unhinging — escalating the physical horror perhaps slightly faster than the plan intended, though this is a minor concern. The repeated prompt line appears three times in the chapter (beginning, middle-end, and final paragraph), which is effective but the middle instance ("I caught her staring at me again. She thought I'd been sleeping." as a section break) feels slightly mechanical. The Marcus-text twist at the end is creative and unexpected — genuinely the chapter's best surprise. It extends the horror's reach without requiring explanation, which is exactly the right instinct. Overall this is confident, controlled horror writing with a strong sense of character and atmosphere. The prose is appropriately plain and human without being dull. The deviations from the plan are mostly improvements or acceptable substitutions. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 8 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 13 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 6

Ok now chapter 6. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
I caught her staring at me again. She thought I’d been sleeping. The black line under her eye was back. It started as a thin thread at the corner, like someone had drawn on her with a Sharpie, then thickened overnight into a sluggish trickle that ran down her cheek and dried in a crust along her jaw. She didn’t wipe it. She never wiped it. When she moved her head the dried stuff flaked off in tiny black scales that caught the morning light like dandruff from hell. I sat across the breakfast table pretending to read a manuscript about Mediterranean diets while she hummed and poured cereal. The folk song had changed. The melody was the same but the words were wrong now, twisted into something that sounded like counting. One, two, three, four—except the numbers stretched and bent until they hurt to hear. She caught me looking at the black stuff and smiled the crooked smile that no longer fit her face. “Messy night,” she said lightly, touching the crust with one fingertip. It came away wet. “You know how I get when I’m worried about you.” I didn’t answer. My throat had closed up days ago. Marcus had stopped texting. The last message three days earlier had been a single thumbs-up emoji. Nothing since. I told myself he was busy. I told myself a lot of things. That afternoon I found the ring. It was on the bathroom counter, spinning loose on the edge of the sink like it had fallen off without her noticing. Claire’s wedding band. The one I’d slid on her finger seven years ago in that tiny courthouse with the ugly fluorescent lights. I picked it up. It was still warm. Inside, where the engraving should have been—*Always, D*—there were tiny scratches that looked like teeth marks. I put it in my pocket and didn’t tell her. The house had started to smell wrong. Not bad exactly. Just… off. Like meat left out too long mixed with the lavender she kept spraying everywhere. The houseplants were dying faster now. The prayer plant she’d brought home had folded its leaves permanently, as if it had given up praying. I watered them anyway. Habit. Daniel habit.
