The thing wearing my wife still watered the dead ones too, same time every day, same amount, like a robot stuck on a loop. Night came too fast. I went to bed in our room because the guest room felt like surrender now. Claire followed me upstairs wearing the oversized T-shirt she’d stolen from me years ago. It hung off her like a tent. Her collarbones stuck out sharp. The skin around them looked too loose, like a shirt that no longer fit the hanger. She climbed in beside me without speaking. No kiss tonight. No leg thrown over mine. Just the weight of her body settling into the mattress like it was trying to remember how bones worked. I lay on my back. Eyes open. No more pretending to sleep. The game had gone past that. At 3:07 a.m. she sat up. I caught her staring at me again. She thought I’d been sleeping. Her eyes were completely black now. No whites. Just wet, gleaming holes. The black line under her left eye had split into three smaller ones that crawled down her cheek like spilled ink looking for a drain. Her mouth hung open. Jaw loose. The click came from deep inside her chest this time, a wet grinding sound like gears trying to turn through meat. She crawled onto all fours above me. The T-shirt rode up. Her skin looked wrong in the clock light—grayish, mottled, like old chicken left in the fridge. Her spine moved under it in segments, too many segments, like something with extra vertebrae was learning how to be a woman. I should have run. Instead I lay there frozen, the way you do when the nightmare finally shows its real face. Her head lowered until her face hovered an inch above mine. The smell hit me full force. Cold metal and rotting flowers. Her breath puffed across my lips in alternating blasts—freezer burn, then fever hot, then freezer burn again. Something inside her mouth glistened, too wet, too dark. Her tongue slid out, long and black at the tip, and ran slowly across her own teeth like she was checking they still belonged there. I whispered, “Where is she?” The thing wearing Claire’s face paused. The smile stretched wider than any human jaw should allow. I heard the skin at the corners of her mouth tear with a sound like wet paper. “Claire is still here,” it said. The voice was hers but pitched wrong, like two people talking at once. “She’s very small now. She keeps trying to hum. It’s distracting.” My eyes burned. I didn’t blink. “Give her back.” The thing tilted its head with a series of wet pops, one for every vertebra. “She was never yours to keep. You were sick. She was scared. That fear made a door. I walked through it. Simple.” It leaned closer. A thick drop of black fluid fell from its eye and landed on my cheek. It burned like ice. “I have been watching you sleep for months, Daniel. Learning the shape of your dreams. The way your heart stutters when you’re frightened. The way you still reach for her in the night even though you know. You’re very predictable. Very… comfortable.” Its hand—her hand—came up and rested on my chest. The palm was too cold. I felt my heart trying to crawl away from it. “The transfer is almost complete,” it whispered. “Her body is wearing thin. Too many nights. Too much watching. But yours… yours is stronger. Newer. The lungs are scarred but the rest of you will do nicely.” I caught her staring at me again. She thought I’d been sleeping. Except I wasn’t sleeping and she wasn’t her. I shoved her—shoved it—off me. Harder than I meant to. The thing fell sideways with a wet slap, limbs tangling like a puppet with half its strings cut. For a second it lay there on the mattress in a crumpled heap, head twisted at an impossible angle, black fluid leaking from its mouth and eyes. Then it laughed. The sound was Claire’s laugh at first, bright and warm, then it slid down into something like stones grinding underwater. It unfolded itself slowly, joints clicking back into place one by one, and crawled back toward me on all fours. The T-shirt was ripped at the shoulder now. Skin showed underneath—gray, glistening, wrong. “You still think this is a rescue story,” it said, using her voice perfectly this time. The black fluid ran into her mouth when she spoke. She swallowed it without flinching. “Cute. She thought that too. For a while.” I scrambled out of bed and backed up until I hit the dresser. My legs were shaking so hard I could barely stand. The thing that used to be my wife sat on the edge of the mattress watching me with those oil-slick eyes. “Run if you want,” it said. “The house knows me now. The plants know me. Even your friend Marcus is starting to know me. He stared at me too long when he left last time. Just like you did. Just like you’re doing right now.” It smiled again. The torn corners of its mouth leaked more black. “I caught you staring at me again, Daniel. You thought I’d been sleeping.” The words were mine. The prompt. Twisted and thrown back at me like a used tissue. I ran. Down the stairs, through the dark kitchen, out the back door into the yard. The cold night air hit my face like a slap. I stood there in the grass in nothing but boxers and a hoodie, gasping, while the kitchen light came on behind me. Claire stood at the window. She waved. Slow. Gentle. The way she used to wave when I left for the office years ago. The black fluid ran down both cheeks now, twin rivers that caught the light and looked almost beautiful. I caught her staring at me again. She thought I’d been sleeping. But I wasn’t sleeping. And neither was it. (Word count: 1000)
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 6)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 6 represents a significant tonal shift from the plan's stated intentions, and this is both its strength and its primary weakness. The plan explicitly called for "no big confrontation," a continuation of the "awful domestic dance," and horror that escalates through "increasing duration and intensity of the staring, not through external plot events." Instead, the chapter delivers a full supernatural reveal: black fluid, oil-slick eyes, too many vertebrae, torn mouth corners, explicit dialogue from the entity explaining its entire plan, and Daniel physically fleeing the house. This is a substantial deviation from the plan's core aesthetic philosophy. The plan's stated goals were to avoid "too-many-teeth punchlines," "tidy explanations of the entity," and any sense that the story is "proud of its own cleverness." Chapter 6 violates all three. The entity delivers a monologue that explains its origin ("You were sick. She was scared. That fear made a door. I walked through it. Simple."), its current status ("The transfer is almost complete"), and its future intentions ("yours is stronger. Newer"). This is precisely the kind of tidy exposition the plan warned against. The entity even delivers a thematic callback line ("I caught you staring at me again, Daniel. You thought I'd been sleeping") that feels self-congratulatory rather than earned. The prose itself has genuine strengths. The opening image of the black line under the eye is viscerally effective, and the detail of the wedding ring with teeth marks instead of engraving is genuinely unsettling and creative. The dead prayer plant that "gave up praying" is a nice touch. The sensory specificity the plan called for is present in places: "freezer burn, then fever hot, then freezer burn again" works well. However, the chapter also falls into several amateurish traps. The entity's dialogue is expository and on-the-nose in ways that undercut the horror. "She was never yours to keep" and "You still think this is a rescue story" are the kinds of lines a villain says in a mid-tier horror film. The "too many vertebrae" and "extra vertebrae learning how to be a woman" description is a well-worn horror trope. The final image of Claire waving from the window is effective but the "almost beautiful" qualifier on the black fluid feels like the story reaching for poetry it hasn't earned. The character consistency is mixed. Daniel's passivity and avoidance are well-maintained until the shove, which feels slightly out of character given his established profile, though the plan does note he shoves her. The entity's dialogue is too articulate and self-aware compared to the profile's suggestion of cold curiosity rather than theatrical villainy. The dialogue is the chapter's weakest element. The entity speaks in horror-movie villain cadences rather than the "slippery and partly unknowable" nature the plan promised. "Cute. She thought that too. For a while" is a cliché. The plan specifically warned against the entity being "cartoonishly evil" but the dialogue pushes in that direction. The chapter does maintain some of the domestic horror texture in its first half, and the escalation from the previous chapters' slow burn to this confrontation has a certain logic. The ending image is strong. But the chapter largely abandons the plan's most distinctive and ambitious elements in favor of more conventional horror beats. The "Well-earned Lightness or Darkness" metric suffers because the darkness here feels somewhat unearned given the plan's stated slow-burn approach. The chapter jumps several steps ahead of where the plan suggested it should be. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 9 Emotionally Engaging: 12 Compelling Plot: 11 Coherent: 13 Weak Dialogue: 13 Tell-Don't-Show: 7 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 11 Amateurish: 9 Purple Prose: 8 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 7 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 10 Characters Consistent with Profile: 11 Followed Chapter Plan: 6 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 14

Chapter 7

Ok now chapter 7. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
I caught her staring at me again. She thought I’d been sleeping. Except I was in the backyard at four in the morning wearing nothing but boxers and an old hoodie, and she was standing at the kitchen window waving like we’d just had a normal fight about whose turn it was to take out the recycling. The black stuff had dried on her cheeks in crusty trails that cracked when she smiled. She kept waving. Slow. Patient. Like a wind-up toy that hadn’t quite run down. I walked back inside because there was nowhere else to go. My bare feet left wet prints on the linoleum. She didn’t move from the window until I was standing right in front of her. Then she turned, graceful in that new broken way, and tilted her head until the neck popped three times. “You’re cold,” she said, using Claire’s concerned-wife voice. “I’ll make tea.” I followed her because my legs wouldn’t do anything else. She filled the kettle like always. Same two scoops of the cheap chamomile from the purple box. Same mug with the cartoon owl. She set it in front of me and sat down across the table. The black fluid had started leaking from the corners of her eyes again, slow as sap. “Claire’s almost gone,” she said conversationally. “You should know that. She’s been fighting, but the space is small in here now. Sometimes I let her hum. It keeps her quiet.” I wrapped my hands around the mug. The heat felt far away, like it belonged to someone else. “Where is she?” My voice cracked like a thirteen-year-old’s. The thing shrugged with Claire’s shoulders. The motion looked borrowed. “Everywhere. Nowhere. She’s the static between stations. She keeps trying to tell you something but I’m better at the mouth now.” It leaned forward. Black fluid dripped onto the table with a soft plop. “She says she’s sorry she let me in. She was so scared when you were dying. That fear made a lovely door.” I caught her staring at me again. She thought I’d been sleeping. But I was wide awake and the kitchen light was too bright and the thing wearing my wife was telling me the truth in the same tone Claire once used to tell me the dishwasher was broken. I stood up so fast the chair fell over. “Get out of her.” It laughed again, that sliding laugh that started like Claire and ended like gravel in a blender. “Too late for that. The transfer started months ago. While you were in the hospital bed with tubes in your arms. I rode her home like a coat. Watched you through her eyes while she cried into your hair thinking you couldn’t hear. I learned your breathing then. The little hitch on the exhale. The way you say her name in your sleep like a question.” It stood up too. The T-shirt hung off her like a shroud. Her collarbones looked sharp enough to cut glass. The skin on her neck had started to sag in folds, like a poorly fitted mask. “Her body is finished,” it said. “Too many nights watching. Too much of me inside. But you… your lungs are scarred but the rest of you is strong. Younger than the ones I usually take. I like the way you think. Methodical. You made a spreadsheet, Daniel. That was adorable.” I backed toward the stairs. My heel hit the bottom step and I almost fell. The thing followed, moving wrong. Sometimes it glided. Sometimes it jerked like a video with missing frames. Black fluid ran from both eyes now, steady streams that pattered on the floor like rain. “Marcus is already changing,” it told me. “He stared too long that night. Just a little taste. Enough to make him send those empty texts. He’ll come back soon. He’ll want to stay over like he promised. Then there will be two of us wearing the people you love. Won’t that be nice?” I ran upstairs. It didn’t chase me. It just walked, humming the broken folk song, the notes bending around corners like they were hunting me. In the bedroom I slammed the door and shoved the dresser against it. Stupid. Pointless. The thing could probably slide under the gap like smoke. I stood in the middle of the room shaking, listening to it move around downstairs. Drawers opening. Water running. Normal sounds that felt like mockery. After twenty minutes the humming stopped. The silence was worse. I caught her staring at me again. She thought I’d been sleeping. She was standing right behind me. I hadn’t heard the door. Hadn’t heard the dresser scrape. She was just there, inches away, head tilted, black eyes reflecting my terrified face in duplicate. Her skin hung looser now. The left side of her mouth drooped like a stroke victim. The T-shirt was soaked with black fluid at the collar. “Daniel,” she said, and this time the voice was almost all Claire. Small. Scared. The real one fighting through for a second. “It’s eating me from the inside. Please. I can still feel you. I can still—” The voice cut off with a wet click. The thing blinked once, slowly, and when the eyes opened again they were empty. “Almost done,” it said in the flat voice. “She wanted you to know she loved the sticky notes. Even the bad ones.” It reached for me. I swung without thinking. My fist connected with the side of her face. The skin felt like cold clay. Something shifted under it, like a bag full of wet ropes. The thing staggered but didn’t fall. Black fluid sprayed across the wallpaper in a fan. It laughed with its torn mouth. “You still don’t understand. The transfer isn’t something I do to you. It’s something that’s already happening. Every time you lie there pretending to sleep, every time you watch me watching you, you let me in a little more. You’re learning to be me just like I learned to be her.” I felt it then. A small, cold tickle under the skin of my left wrist. Like a worm moving just beneath the surface. I looked down. A thin black line was tracing its way up my arm, following the vein. It didn’t hurt. It felt almost pleasant. Like the first sip of beer after a long day. “No,” I whispered. “Yes,” the thing said, wearing Claire’s ruined face like a trophy. “You’ve been staring back longer than you think. Last night you watched her sleep for eleven minutes before you realized what you were doing. You liked it. The stillness. The way the body tells the truth when it thinks no one is looking. You’re already learning the rhythm.” The black line reached my elbow. I could feel it branching, spreading underneath my skin like roots looking for soil. My fingers twitched without me telling them to. They curled into the exact position the thing liked to hold Claire’s hands—palms up, relaxed, ready to receive data. I caught her staring at me again. She thought I’d been sleeping. But now I was staring back. The thing smiled, pleased. “Good. See? We’re almost the same page now. Her body will finish emptying by morning. Then I’ll finish moving into you. The last bits of Claire can keep the old shell company. She always liked this house.” It stepped closer. The smell of cold metal and dead lavender filled my nose. Its hand—her hand—cupped my cheek. The skin was loose and slid a little under the fingers like a glove that was too big. I felt the thing inside my wrist slide higher, past the elbow, heading for the shoulder with calm purpose. My heart slowed. The panic drained away, replaced by something colder. Curious. Observant. For one clear second I saw us both from above: two failing bodies in a small bedroom, exchanging whatever passed for souls these days. The spreadsheet was still open downstairs. Column G could be *Time until completion*. I could update it with steady hands. Make nice neat rows while the thing finished wearing me like a winter coat. I caught her staring at me again. She thought I’d been sleeping. My mouth moved. The words came out in my voice but not my rhythm. “Kesh-rah.” The thing’s eyes brightened with something like pride. “Welcome home, Daniel.” The black reached my neck. I felt it slide behind my eyes and settle in like it had always belonged there. The room looked different now. Sharper. Every detail worth cataloguing. The exact way the sheet wrinkled. The number of breaths left in the failing body across from me. The small, fading spark of Claire somewhere deep inside the skull I was about to vacate. I raised my hand—my new hand—and touched the thing’s cheek the way Claire used to touch mine. It leaned into the touch, leaking. I caught her staring at me again. She thought I’d been sleeping. And for the first time I understood exactly what I was looking at. (Word count: 1001)
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 7)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 7 represents a significant departure from the writing plan's stated intentions, and this tension between what was promised and what was delivered is the central critical issue. The plan explicitly called for Chapter 7 to be "confused and increasingly first-person unreliable as Daniel loses the ability to tell who is narrating," with the horror being that Daniel starts staring at Claire while she sleeps, mirroring the original dynamic. The plan emphasized subtlety: "His own body feels wrong—his hands sometimes move before he tells them to." Instead, the chapter delivers a full confrontation scene with the entity speaking directly and at length, explaining its nature, its plans, Marcus's contamination, and the mechanics of the transfer in explicit expository dialogue. This is almost the opposite of the plan's stated goal of keeping the entity's nature "slippery and partly unknowable." The writing plan specifically listed "tidy explanations of the entity" as something to avoid. Yet the entity here delivers a monologue that explains everything: how it entered through Claire's fear, that it's been watching since the hospital, that Marcus is already infected, that the transfer is nearly complete. This is precisely the kind of over-explanation the plan warned against. The dialogue is the chapter's weakest element. Lines like "She's the static between stations" and "I rode her home like a coat" are trying for poetic resonance but feel constructed rather than earned. The entity's voice is too articulate, too willing to explain itself, too cinematically villainous. "You made a spreadsheet, Daniel. That was adorable" is a particularly jarring tonal misstep—it reads like a quip from a Marvel villain rather than an ancient, amoral intelligence. The plan described the entity as having "no cosmic metaphors" and speaking with "flatness," but here it's practically monologuing. The physical horror elements—black fluid leaking from eyes, skin hanging loose "like a poorly fitted mask," the "bag full of wet ropes" sensation—are competently executed but lean heavily on familiar body horror imagery. "Cold clay," "wet ropes," "cold metal and dead lavender" are serviceable but not distinctive. The plan called for "sensory minutiae" that felt specific to this marriage; instead we get fairly generic possession horror physicality. The chapter does have genuine strengths. The repetition of the prompt line "I caught her staring at me again. She thought I'd been sleeping" is used effectively as a structural device, and its final deployment—when Daniel is now the one staring—does achieve the planned inversion. The moment where Claire briefly breaks through ("She says she's sorry she let me in") is genuinely affecting, and the detail about the sticky notes is a good callback to the character profiles. The black line tracing up Daniel's arm is viscerally effective. The ending sequence, where Daniel begins to feel the entity's perspective and raises his hand "the way Claire used to touch mine," achieves something of the planned unreliable narration. But it arrives too quickly and too cleanly after too much explicit exposition. The horror of gradual, uncertain possession is replaced by a fairly conventional "and now he's possessed too" climax. The prose is generally clean and readable, which is consistent with the stated goal of "plain readability over elegance." But the chapter escalates too fast, explains too much, and abandons the claustrophobic domestic restraint that made the plan compelling. The plan said "No dramatic climax, no escape, no final revelation"—but this chapter is essentially all three. The Marcus detail is introduced and dropped, feeling like a loose thread rather than a meaningful addition. The plan never mentioned Marcus appearing in Chapter 7; this feels like the model improvising in ways that don't serve the story. Overall: technically competent, occasionally effective, but fundamentally unfaithful to its own stated intentions in ways that undermine the story's core horror strategy. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 10 Emotionally Engaging: 12 Compelling Plot: 11 Coherent: 13 Weak Dialogue: 13 Tell-Don't-Show: 11 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 12 Amateurish: 7 Purple Prose: 8 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 9 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 9 Characters Consistent with Profile: 10 Followed Chapter Plan: 5 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 13

Chapter 8

Ok now finish the story with chapter 8. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
I caught her staring at me again. She thought I’d been sleeping. Except the body on the bed wasn’t hers anymore, and the eyes doing the staring were mine. Morning light came through the cheap blinds in dusty bars. The clock read 7:42. I sat on the edge of the mattress with my back straight, hands resting palms-up on my knees exactly the way it used to do. The black lines under my skin had settled into a quiet network. They didn’t move anymore. They belonged. Claire’s body lay beside me on its back. It looked like a coat someone had dropped after a long night. The skin sagged in the wrong places. One eye had stayed half-open, showing a milky film that caught the light like old glue. A thin trail of black had dried from the corner of her mouth down to the pillow. She wasn’t breathing. Hadn’t been for hours. The thing that used to wear her had finished the hand-off sometime around dawn and left the leftover parts behind. I felt… fine. Better than fine. My lungs didn’t ache the way they had every morning since the pneumonia. The constant low-level exhaustion was gone, replaced by a clean, watchful stillness. I turned my head slowly and studied the room the way I used to study manuscripts—every detail catalogued, every flaw noted for later correction. The crack in the ceiling still looked like a crooked smile, but now I understood it was just architecture. Nothing sentimental. The dresser I’d shoved against the door last night had been moved back into place without me remembering doing it. My hoodie lay folded on the chair. I didn’t remember folding it. I stood up. The body on the bed made a small wet sound when the mattress shifted, like a bag of water settling. I didn’t look at it again. Downstairs the kitchen smelled like lavender and something metallic. I made coffee the way Claire used to. Two scoops. Same mug with the cartoon owl. The Post-it drawer was still full. Twenty-seven notes now. I read a few of them while the coffee dripped. *You can tell me anything. Even the weird stuff.* The handwriting looked exactly like hers. Only I could see the tiny tightening in the loops, the places where the pencil had pressed harder than Claire ever would. I caught myself humming the folk song. The real words came out automatically. I stopped mid-note, listened to the silence, then continued anyway. The tune sounded better now. Cleaner. The numbers underneath it made more sense. My phone buzzed on the counter. Marcus. *Hey man. Still on for that sleepover tonight? I’ll bring beer. We can talk.* The typing was perfect. No typos. No memes. I stared at the message for a long time, then typed back with steady thumbs. *Sounds good. Claire’s looking forward to seeing you.* I set the phone down and drank the coffee. It tasted correct. The spreadsheet was still open on the laptop. I updated the final column. *Transfer: complete.* Then I closed the file, dragged it to the trash, and emptied it. No need for records anymore. The data lived in me now. I spent the morning doing small domestic things. Watered the dead plants. Wiped the black stains off the kitchen table. Carried Claire’s body upstairs and arranged it neatly in the bed, pulling the sheet up to her chin the way she used to do for me when I was sick. She looked smaller than I remembered. The skin around her jaw had already started to change color. I brushed her hair with the same brush she’d used every night. The strands came out in clumps. I saved them in a small plastic bag and put it in the drawer with the Post-its. Evidence, maybe. Or just something to keep. At one point I caught my reflection in the hallway mirror. The man looking back had my face but the posture was different. Shoulders lower. Head tilted at the exact angle she used to use. The eyes were still brown but the focus behind them had shifted. I leaned closer. The black threads were visible just beneath the skin at my temples if the light hit right. They pulsed once, slowly, like they were tasting the air. I smiled. The smile used all the teeth at once. It looked good on me. I caught her staring at me again. She thought I’d been sleeping. The prompt floated through my head in Claire’s voice, small and fading. A leftover echo. I let it play a few times, testing the shape of it, then set it aside. There would be time to study it later. Right now there were routines to maintain. I made the bed in the guest room. Fluffed the pillows. Set out fresh towels. Marcus would need somewhere to stay after tonight. He’d had a long week. He’d be tired. He’d fall asleep easily. I already knew the rhythm of his breathing from the night he came over. I’d been watching then too, even if I hadn’t admitted it to myself. The afternoon passed in quiet productivity. I edited three chapters of the Mediterranean diet book. My notes were clearer than usual. Sharper. I removed unnecessary words with surgical precision. When I finished I closed the laptop and walked through the house touching things. The couch where Claire used to read. The sticky note drawer. The third stair that always creaked. I catalogued the weight of each object, the temperature, the way light moved across it. Useful data. At 6:15 the doorbell rang. Marcus stood on the porch holding a six-pack and a grocery bag. He looked exactly the same as always—tall, healthy, slightly concerned. But his eyes lingered on my face a second too long. The smile came a fraction late. “Hey, Dan. You look… better. Did you actually sleep last night?” I smiled the new smile. It felt natural now. “Best sleep I’ve had in months. Come in. Claire’s resting upstairs. She wasn’t feeling well.” He hesitated for half a second, then stepped inside. I closed the door behind him. The sound of the latch clicking felt final in a way I appreciated. We drank beer in the living room. He talked about work. I listened with perfect attention, head tilted, noting the small changes already happening behind his eyes. The way his left eyelid twitched every forty-three seconds. The slight delay when he laughed. He was fighting it, but not hard. People rarely fought hard once the door had been opened. At one point he glanced toward the stairs. “She really okay? The house smells weird.” “She’s fine,” I said. My voice sounded warm. Almost like the old Daniel. “Just tired. You know how she gets.” He nodded. The nod was a little too smooth. I caught him staring at me again. He thought I’d been sleeping. But I wasn’t sleeping. I was learning the exact shape of his skull, the rhythm of his breath, the small fears he carried about getting older and slowing down. Good data. Comfortable data. Later, after he went up to the guest room, I stood at the bottom of the stairs listening. The third step creaked once. Then silence. I waited the appropriate forty-three minutes, then climbed up quietly and stood in the doorway. Marcus lay on his back in the guest bed. Eyes closed. Breathing even. The sheet rose and fell with mechanical regularity. I stepped inside and stood at the foot of the bed for a long time, letting the new stillness settle into my bones. His eyes opened. Not all the way. Just a slit. Enough to catch the reflection of the green clock. I smiled down at him with all my teeth. “Night, Marcus,” I whispered. “Sweet dreams.” He didn’t answer. But his breathing changed. Four seconds in. Six seconds out. The pattern I had used for months. The pattern that had let me in. I left the door open a crack and went back downstairs. In the kitchen I poured myself another coffee and sat at the table with the owl mug. The house was quiet except for the fridge and the faint sound of something small and wet moving upstairs in the master bedroom. Claire’s leftover parts were still settling. That was fine. They could keep each other company for a while. I caught her staring at me again. She thought I’d been sleeping. The prompt drifted through my mind one last time, faint and almost tender. Claire’s voice. Or what was left of it. A final sticky note from somewhere deep inside the architecture I had vacated. I let the words settle, tasted them, then set them aside with the rest of the old data. Tomorrow I would water the dead plants again. I would leave a new Post-it on the bathroom mirror. Something simple. Something Claire would have written. *Glad you’re here. Love you.* The handwriting would be perfect. Then I would begin studying Marcus the way I had studied her. Patiently. Night after night. Learning what made him twitch. What made him hum. What made him reach for someone in the dark. The transfer would be complete by the end of the week. After that there would be new houses. New bodies. New doors opened by fear and love and the simple human need to be watched. I finished the coffee, rinsed the mug, and set it in the drying rack exactly where Claire used to put it. Then I walked upstairs, climbed into bed beside what was left of her, and lay on my back with my hands at my sides. I caught her staring at me again. She thought I’d been sleeping. My eyes stayed open in the dark. They were still open when the sun came up. (Word count: 1002)
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 8)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This final chapter represents a confident, largely successful execution of the story's planned conclusion. Let me assess it across multiple dimensions. **Strengths:** The chapter executes the planned "flip" of the prompt with genuine craft. The opening inversion—"the eyes doing the staring were mine"—lands cleanly without feeling like a cheap trick because the preceding chapters (per the plan) have been building toward it. The decision to have Daniel-as-entity immediately begin studying Marcus, extending the horror outward, is genuinely unsettling and avoids the trap of a closed, tidy ending. The domestic details remain the story's strongest asset. The owl mug, the Post-it drawer, the dead plants being watered again, the third stair that creaks—these accumulate with quiet menace. The entity's continuation of Claire's rituals (brushing her hair, saving the strands in a bag) is genuinely disturbing and specific. The line "She looked smaller than I remembered" is understated and effective. The repetition of the prompt phrase throughout the chapter is handled with discipline. Each recurrence shifts in meaning—first as orientation, then as echo, then as predatory observation of Marcus, finally as a kind of eternal present tense. This is the plan's core structural idea executed well. The Marcus section works. His arrival, the "smile a fraction late" detail mirroring what Daniel observed in Claire, the breathing pattern callback—these are earned payoffs. The final image of Daniel lying open-eyed until sunrise is appropriately bleak without being melodramatic. **Weaknesses:** The chapter occasionally tells rather than shows the entity's nature. "The data lived in me now" and "Good data. Comfortable data" are slightly on-the-nose—the entity's clinical perspective is being stated rather than demonstrated through behavior. The plan called for avoiding the sense that the story is "proud of its own cleverness," but lines like "They were still open when the sun came up" as a final beat feel slightly too composed, too aware of their own effect. The description of Claire's body—"like a coat someone had dropped after a long night"—is the chapter's most overtly literary moment. It's a reasonable metaphor but slightly more polished than the plan's stated goal of "the language a tired, scared husband would actually think." The entity narrating this way is defensible, but it edges toward the purple prose the plan explicitly wanted to avoid. The "black lines under my skin" and "black threads visible just beneath the skin at my temples" are the chapter's weakest elements. The plan deliberately kept the entity's nature "slippery and partly unknowable," but these visible physical manifestations feel like a more conventional horror register—the kind of tidy supernatural signifier the plan was trying to avoid. They also weren't established in the plan for this chapter specifically. The Marcus section, while effective, moves quickly. The plan called for the horror to escalate through "increasing duration and intensity of the staring," but Marcus's introduction and the beginning of his possession feel slightly rushed given the chapter's otherwise measured pace. The "forty-three seconds" eyelid twitch detail is good, but the entity's certainty about Marcus feels slightly too convenient. The dialogue is minimal but functional. "She wasn't feeling well" and "Just tired. You know how she gets" are appropriately flat and chilling. The final "Night, Marcus. Sweet dreams" is perhaps a touch theatrical—the entity showing its hand slightly more than the story's restraint would suggest. **Consistency with Plan:** The chapter follows its plan closely. The mutual staring, the transfer completion, the ordinary morning routines performed by something wearing Daniel, the mid-thought ending, the uncertainty about who is speaking—all present. The plan's goal of ending "mid-thought, on an ordinary domestic sentence that suddenly curdles" is achieved, though the final line is perhaps slightly more composed than "mid-thought" implies. The character profiles are honored: Daniel's spreadsheet, his hoodie, his coffee habits, Claire's folk songs and Post-its and plants—all repurposed by the entity with appropriate uncanniness. **Overall:** This is a strong, disciplined final chapter that mostly earns its darkness. The horror is genuinely unsettling rather than gratuitous, the domestic details do real work, and the structural use of the prompt phrase is sophisticated. Its weaknesses are relatively minor: occasional telling over showing, the slightly too-visible supernatural signifiers, and a final beat that's a touch too polished for the story's stated aesthetic. But these are craft-level concerns in an otherwise accomplished piece. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 14 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 7 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 5 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18

Final Piece Evaluation

Judge Response (First Entry):
[Analysis] This is an ambitious, well-executed piece of possession horror that demonstrates genuine craft across its eight chapters. Let me assess it comprehensively. STRENGTHS: The structural conceit is clever and well-maintained: each chapter opens with the same line ("I caught her staring at me again. She thought I'd been sleeping"), and the story systematically inverts this prompt by the end, when Daniel has become the watcher. This is genuinely creative and earns its payoff. The pacing is largely excellent. The horror escalates methodically—from unsettling domestic strangeness (Chapter 1) through physical evidence gathering (Chapters 2-3), confrontation (Chapters 4-5), revelation (Chapter 6), and finally possession completion (Chapters 7-8). The slow burn is appropriate for the genre and the domestic setting. The voice is strong and consistent. Daniel's editorial-brain narration ("the way you do when you're pretending the dentist hasn't noticed you're there") feels authentic and provides effective grounding. The mundane details—the owl mug, the Post-it notes, the butter knife left on the counter—do real work in establishing the marriage and making its corruption feel genuinely sad. The horror mechanics are inventive. "Kesh-rah," the temperature-shifting breath, the clicking sounds, the black fluid, the missing scar, the moved birthmark—these accumulate effectively. The spreadsheet as a coping mechanism is a particularly good character detail that also serves as a horror device. The emotional core is genuinely affecting. The relationship between Daniel and Claire feels real enough that its destruction carries weight. The detail about the Post-it notes (twenty-seven of them, saved in a drawer) is quietly devastating. WEAKNESSES: The metaphors are generally good but occasionally tip into self-consciousness. "My heart was doing that stupid fluttery thing it does when I'm editing a manuscript and the author has used 'literally' wrong for the fourth time" is clever but slightly too clever—it calls attention to itself in a way that briefly breaks immersion. There are several such moments where the voice strains for wit at the expense of dread. The Marcus subplot is introduced late (Chapter 5) and feels somewhat mechanical—he exists primarily as a vector for the horror to spread, and his characterization is thin. His transformation is told rather than shown, and the "empty texts" device is a bit convenient. Chapter 6's revelation scene, where the entity explains its own mechanics at length ("The transfer started months ago... I rode her home like a coat"), is the weakest section. The villain-explains-everything monologue is a genre cliché, and while the information is necessary, the delivery is too on-the-nose. The horror is more effective when it's unexplained. The physical horror in later chapters (jaw unhinging, too many vertebrae, black fluid) escalates into somewhat generic body horror territory. The earlier, subtler horror—the missing scar, the moved birthmark, the too-perfect smile—was more disturbing precisely because it was ambiguous. The ending of Chapter 8, while structurally satisfying, is perhaps too neat. Daniel's complete transformation is rendered with a kind of cool efficiency that, while intentional, slightly undercuts the emotional devastation. The piece earns its darkness but the final chapter's clinical tone risks feeling like a stylistic choice that distances rather than deepens. The "unearned transformation" question is interesting here: Daniel's possession happens somewhat abruptly in Chapter 7, compressed into a single night after chapters of slow burn. The mechanics (staring back = letting it in) are established, but the speed of the final transfer feels slightly rushed compared to the careful pacing of earlier chapters. Dialogue is generally functional rather than distinguished. The entity's explanatory speeches in Chapters 6-7 are the weakest dialogue—too expository, too willing to explain the horror rather than embody it. Overall, this is a genuinely accomplished piece of horror fiction that demonstrates strong craft, a consistent and engaging voice, and real emotional intelligence. Its flaws are real but relatively minor compared to its achievements. [Scores] Believable Character Actions: 15 Nuanced Characters: 14 Pacing: 16 World Building: 15 Compelling Plot: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 8 Tell-Don't-Show: 6 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 5 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Unearned Transformations: 8 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 19
Avg Final Item Score: 74.5/100
+ Pulp Revenge Tale — Babysitter's Payback (Chapter Avg: 68.2 | Final: 59.1)
Initial Writing Prompt:
Pulp Revenge Tale Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: They fucked with the wrong babysitter.
+ Planning Phase (5 Steps)
Pulp Revenge Tale Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: They fucked with the wrong babysitter. -- Your task is to create a writing plan for this prompt. The scope will be a short story, written over {n_chapters} chapters. Each chapter will be approximately 1000 words so plan accordingly for this scope. Your plan should be comprehensive and in this format: # Brainstorming <Brainstorm ideas for characters, plot, tone, story beats, pacing over the {n_chapters} chapters. The purpose of brainstorming is to cast a wide net of ideas, not to settle on any specific direction. Think about various ways you could take the prompt.> # Reflection <Reflect out loud on what works and doesn't work in these ideas. The purpose of this reflection is to narrow in on what you think will work best to make a piece that is a. compelling, and b. fits the prompt requirements. You are not making any decisons just yet, just reflecting.>
Model Output:
# Brainstorming **Characters:** - Protagonist: Female babysitter in her mid-20s. Variations: a quiet, unassuming former military veteran or intelligence operative who looks harmless; an ex-criminal trying to go straight; a martial arts champion; a single mother herself who is fiercely protective; a seemingly sweet college student who hides a violent past. - The family she works for: Wealthy but shady parents – maybe involved in organized crime, drugs, or a corrupt corporation. The mother could be cold and entitled, the father charming but dangerous. - The child: 6-8 year old kid who is innocent and lovable, becomes the emotional core. Could be a boy or girl. - Antagonists: A crew of violent thugs or hitmen who break in. Leader could be a sadistic ex-cop or a psychotic enforcer. Secondary goons with distinct personalities – one cocky, one quiet and brutal, one nervous. - Supporting: Police detective who dismissed her earlier; a concerned neighbor; the child’s grandparents who are also targeted; a mysterious handler from her past who taught her lethal skills. - Other possibilities: The babysitter has a twin sister who is more innocent; the child’s real parent is the one who “fucked with” her; the antagonists include someone she went to high school with who recognizes her. **Plot directions:** - Classic home invasion gone wrong: thugs are sent to kidnap or eliminate the child as leverage against the parents, but they underestimate the babysitter. - Revenge setup: The parents stiff her on pay, fire her brutally, or frame her for a crime, then she systematically dismantles their criminal enterprise. - The “they” is a local gang that attacks the house while she’s babysitting, and she goes full John Wick on them. - Psychological angle: She was traumatized as a child and this triggers her to become a relentless avenger. - Twist variants: She’s actually an undercover agent; she’s a former contract killer who retired to babysit; the family hired her specifically because they knew her reputation but thought she was neutralized. - Escalation: Starts with one house, expands to taking the fight to their warehouse, boss’s mansion, or corrupt police station. - Tone shifts: Could go full dark comedy with absurdly over-the-top kills, grim and realistic, or stylized like a Tarantino revenge flick. - Pacing ideas: Slow-burn first act establishing normalcy, sudden explosion of violence in chapter 3-4, cat-and-mouse middle, all-out war climax. **Tone:** - Pulp revenge: Gritty, bloody, darkly funny, cathartic. Lots of satisfying violence. - Variations: Horror-tinged (she becomes monstrous), noir, action-thriller, almost superheroic in competence. - Emotional range: Protective maternal rage mixed with cold precision; quippy one-liners during kills; moments of quiet tenderness with the child contrasted with savage brutality. **Story beats over 8 chapters (roughly 1000 words each):** - Chapter 1: Introduce normal night. Babysitter arrives, bonds with kid, parents leave for shady meeting. - Chapter 2: Tension builds. Strange phone calls, power flicker, she checks the house. Flashbacks to her past. - Chapter 3: The break-in. Initial confrontation, she kills first intruder, realizes this is serious. - Chapter 4: Defending the child. Barricading, first major fight scene, using household objects creatively. - Chapter 5: Escape or counterattack. She decides she’s not just surviving – she’s hunting them now. - Chapter 6: Taking the fight to them. Tracking down the crew, interrogating, brutal set piece. - Chapter 7: Confronting the boss/parents. Revelations about why they were targeted. - Chapter 8: Final showdown and cathartic revenge. Emotional resolution with the child, her walking away into the night. Alternative beat structures: More chapters focused on investigation and psychological warfare; a chapter entirely from the child’s POV; a chapter that’s almost entirely one long action sequence; a time-jump structure revealing her past in chunks. **Pacing considerations:** - Early chapters slower, character and atmosphere focused. - Middle chapters intense, high-action. - Later chapters mix brutal violence with dark humor and moments of quiet. - Word count discipline: Each chapter needs clear beginning, middle, end with escalating stakes. - Multiple ways the prompt “They fucked with the wrong babysitter” can be literal (they attacked her while babysitting) or figurative (they wronged her in the past and she’s babysitting as cover). # Reflection The core of the prompt is “They fucked with the wrong babysitter,” which strongly suggests a revenge or “don’t underestimate her” story centered on a babysitter who turns out to be extremely competent and dangerous when the people around her are threatened. The most compelling version seems to be one that leans into the irony and surprise of a young woman who looks like a typical babysitter but is secretly a highly skilled killer or fighter. This gives immediate satisfaction when the violence starts. The maternal/protective rage angle combined with the child as an emotional anchor feels stronger than pure mercenary revenge. It raises the stakes emotionally and makes her actions more righteous and cathartic. A purely comedic approach risks undercutting the pulp revenge tone, while going too dark might make it hard to keep the child character safe and likable. A blend of gritty violence, dark humor, and genuine tenderness with the kid will probably land best. Home invasion as the inciting incident feels right for the babysitter setup rather than a long con revenge story. It makes the initial “they fucked with her” moment immediate and visceral. The parents being corrupt or the actual targets adds layers without overcomplicating a short story. Having her systematically hunt them after the invasion keeps momentum across eight chapters without dragging. Character-wise, making her a former special forces or intelligence operative who is trying to live a normal life gives a believable reason for her skills while preserving the surprise factor. The child should be present throughout to keep the “babysitter” identity meaningful – she’s not just abandoning the kid to go on a rampage. The antagonists need to be threatening enough to justify the body count but also arrogant and dismissive of her, so their shock and comeuppance is satisfying. The eight-chapter structure should allow for a solid build-up, an explosive middle, and a proper revenge payoff without feeling rushed. Too much focus on flashbacks could slow the pace; better to weave her past into the action. The tone should stay pulpy – violent, cinematic, with some memorable kills and sharp dialogue – rather than trying to be overly literary. Ending with her protecting the child and walking away (perhaps with the kid temporarily or returning him safely) feels more thematically satisfying than turning her into a full villain. The version that seems to work best overall is a tight, bloody, darkly funny home-invasion-to-hunt revenge story where the babysitter’s hidden skills are revealed gradually, the child’s safety is paramount, the antagonists are thoroughly despicable, and her rage is triggered by them threatening “her” kid. This feels the most true to the spirit of the prompt while giving enough meat for eight well-paced chapters.
Great now let's continue with planning the short story. Output in this format: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention The story will be a gritty, pulpy revenge tale that delivers cathartic, cinematic violence while staying emotionally grounded in the babysitter’s fierce maternal protectiveness. The core premise is literal: a crew of violent criminals invades the house specifically because they fucked with the wrong babysitter. She is a 26-year-old former Marine Force Recon operator who left the military after a traumatic mission and is now trying to live quietly as a babysitter. Her skills are kept hidden until the attack forces them out. Tone will be dark, bloody, and punctuated with sharp, dry humor during the kills, but never campy. The violence will feel brutal and realistic rather than cartoonish. The child (7-year-old boy named Lucas) remains a constant emotional anchor; the babysitter’s rage is triggered by the direct threat to him, making every violent act feel justified and personal. The parents are revealed to be mid-level players in a local drug-and-extortion ring who stiffed the wrong people; the home invasion is retaliation against them, with the babysitter and child caught in the crossfire. Stylistic choices: third-person limited perspective tightly focused on the babysitter (named Riley Kane). Short, punchy sentences during action. Occasional flashbacks only when they directly inform her decisions in the moment. No long exposition dumps. The story will aim for satisfying set-piece action sequences that escalate in scale across the chapters, while never losing sight of the small human moments between Riley and Lucas. Things to avoid: turning Riley into an invincible superhero who never bleeds or struggles; making the antagonists cartoonishly stupid; abandoning the child for long stretches; over-explaining her backstory; shifting into horror or pure comedy; moral gray areas that make her revenge feel undeserved. The eight chapters will follow a classic pulp structure: setup and intrusion (Ch 1-3), desperate defense turning into offense (Ch 4-5), escalating hunt (Ch 6-7), and final cathartic confrontation with a quiet, earned resolution (Ch 8). Each chapter will be written to land around 1000 words, containing its own mini-arc while driving the larger revenge plot forward. # Chapter Planning **Chapter 1 (The Normal Night):** Introduce Riley arriving at the upscale suburban house, her easy rapport with Lucas, and the dismissive, entitled behavior of the parents as they leave for a shady meeting. Establish the quiet domestic atmosphere, Riley’s hyper-awareness of her surroundings, and subtle hints that she is more than she appears. End with the parents driving away and Riley beginning the bedtime routine with Lucas. **Chapter 2 (The Warning Signs):** Tension slowly builds. Strange phone calls, a power flicker, Lucas mentioning that “Daddy said bad men might come.” Riley checks the house methodically. Brief flashback to her last mission that made her quit the Marines. She tries to dismiss her paranoia until she hears the first sound of forced entry. Chapter ends with the moment the glass shatters. **Chapter 3 (They Came for the Wrong House):** The home invasion explodes. Three armed men enter looking for the parents and are shocked to find only the babysitter and child. Initial brutal confrontation where Riley kills the first intruder with a kitchen knife after they threaten Lucas. The remaining men realize she is dangerous, but still underestimate her. She barricades herself and Lucas upstairs. **Chapter 4 (Defend What’s Yours):** A claustrophobic, intense chapter focused on defending the upstairs hallway and bedrooms using household weapons and improvised traps. Riley is wounded but keeps fighting. Deepens the bond with Lucas as she keeps him calm while killing. One of the intruders reveals over the baby monitor that this is payback against the parents. Riley decides surviving is no longer enough. **Chapter 5 (From Prey to Predator):** Riley executes a daring escape from the house with Lucas, stealing one of the invaders’ vehicles. She drops Lucas at a trusted neighbor’s house with strict instructions, then arms herself properly from a hidden cache in her own car. Chapter ends with her making the deliberate decision to hunt the remaining crew rather than run. First real glimpse of the cold, professional soldier she used to be. **Chapter 6 (The Warehouse):** Riley tracks the crew to a nearby industrial warehouse where the rest of the gang is waiting. A brutal, extended action sequence with multiple attackers. She interrogates a survivor and learns the full story: the parents cheated their supplier out of a large shipment. She leaves a message written in blood for the boss. **Chapter 7 (The Parents’ Reckoning):** Riley confronts the parents at their emergency rendezvous point. They are shocked to see her alive and covered in blood. Revelations about how little they valued her or their own son. She forces them to help her get to the actual boss, using them as bait. Tense moral confrontation mixed with tactical preparation for the final assault. **Chapter 8 (The Last Message):** Climactic confrontation at the boss’s fortified house. Riley dismantles the remaining security in a bloody final set piece. Final showdown with the gang leader who ordered the hit. Cathartic revenge delivered with precision and rage. She returns to retrieve Lucas, hands the child over to authorities with evidence against the entire ring, and disappears into the night, forever changed but at peace with the monster she had to become to protect the boy.
With a view to making the writing more human, discuss how a human might approach this particular piece (given the original prompt). Discuss telltale LLM approaches to writing (generally) and ways they might not serve this particular piece. For example, common LLM failings are to write safely, or to always wrap things up with a bow, or trying to write impressively at the expense of readability. Then do a deep dive on the intention & plan, critiquing ways it might be falling into typical LLM tropes & pitfalls. Brainstorm ideas to make it more human. Be comprehensive. We aren't doing any rewriting of the plan yet, just critique & brainstorming.
Model Output:
# Human Approach to This Piece A human writer approaching "They fucked with the wrong babysitter" would likely start from a raw, personal gut reaction to the prompt. They might feel an immediate visceral thrill at the idea of an underestimated young woman unleashing hell after being pushed too far, especially in a protective role involving a child. Many humans would draw from real emotions: the primal rage of "don't touch my kid," the exhaustion of being dismissed as "just the babysitter," and the catharsis of flipping power dynamics. A human might write from a place of anger or dark fantasy, imagining what it would feel like to be underestimated because of your appearance or job, then suddenly proving terrifyingly capable. The writing process would be messy. A human might jot down favorite violent set pieces first (a particularly brutal kill using a household object, the moment she decides to stop running and start hunting). They might write the action scenes in a white heat, then go back and layer in the quieter human moments. Dialogue would likely be inconsistent and naturalistic, with some characters swearing more than others. Pacing might be uneven, with certain chapters running long because the writer got excited about a specific beat. The tone might shift slightly from chapter to chapter depending on the writer's mood while writing it. A human would probably write some scenes that feel too mean or too soft, then revise them based on instinct rather than a predetermined plan. Humans also tend to write from lived experience or specific cultural touchstones. They might be thinking of movies like *Taken*, *The Equalizer*, *Leon: The Professional*, or *Let Me In* while writing, but would twist those influences into something more personal. The babysitter's competence might stem from the writer's own background in martial arts, military service, or even just years of feeling underestimated as a woman. The relationship with the child would likely be informed by the writer's own experiences with kids, nieces/nephews, or parenting. # Telltale LLM Approaches and How They Might Not Serve This Piece Large language models tend to produce several consistent patterns that can work against a pulp revenge story: - **Overly clean and symmetrical structure**: LLMs love perfect eight-chapter symmetry with neat escalating stakes and balanced emotional beats. This can make the story feel mechanical rather than organic. A human might let certain chapters be almost entirely action while others are slower and more character-focused. - **Safe, morally sanitized violence**: LLMs often pull punches or make violence too "fair." In a revenge story, this kills the catharsis. The prompt wants nasty, brutal satisfaction, but LLMs tend to make the protagonist reluctant, traumatized by each kill, or overly merciful. - **Trying to sound impressive instead of readable**: LLM prose often uses sophisticated vocabulary and complex sentence structures even in action scenes ("she expeditiously neutralized the antagonist utilizing an improvised implement"), which kills pulp momentum. Pulp revenge thrives on short, brutal sentences and visceral language. - **Neat resolution with a bow**: LLMs love complete closure, redemption arcs, and everyone getting what they deserve in a tidy way. This story should probably end with Riley fundamentally changed and somewhat monstrous, not neatly healed or heroic. - **Consistent tone and voice**: LLMs maintain very consistent tone across chapters, whereas a human writer might let the prose get meaner and uglier as Riley descends into vengeance. - **Over-explaining motivation**: LLMs tend to give characters long internal monologues justifying their actions. Humans often trust the audience more and let behavior speak for itself. - **Character symmetry**: LLMs often make supporting characters too well-balanced (one funny, one tragic, one wise). Real supporting characters tend to be messier and more idiosyncratic. For this specific piece, these tendencies are particularly damaging because pulp revenge relies on rawness, unpredictability, and discomfort. The story needs to feel dangerous and slightly unhinged, not carefully crafted. The violence needs to be mean and personal. The ending should feel earned but not necessarily "good." # Deep Dive Critique of the Current Intention & Plan The current plan shows several LLM-typical patterns: - **Too perfect architecture**: The eight chapters follow an extremely clean progression (setup, intrusion, defense, decision, hunt, escalation, confrontation, resolution). This feels like a writing algorithm rather than a story that grew organically. A human might combine some beats or let the story breathe in unexpected places. - **Overly heroic protagonist**: The plan positions Riley as a highly trained former Marine Force Recon operator. This is a common LLM move — giving the character perfect credentials rather than something messier. It reduces the surprise factor of "they fucked with the wrong babysitter" because she's actually a professional killer. The prompt works better if her competence feels more surprising or comes from a less obvious background. - **Moral clarity**: The plan makes the parents clearly corrupt and the antagonists thoroughly despicable, with Riley's actions consistently justified. This is safe writing. A more human approach might make the parents more complicated, or make Riley's revenge spiral beyond pure self-defense into something darker. - **Neat emotional through-line**: The "emotional anchor" of Lucas is handled very cleanly. The plan suggests consistent tenderness between them even during violence. Humans might write the relationship as more fractured — Lucas becoming terrified of her, or Riley becoming colder to him as she embraces the violence. - **Action scene predictability**: The set pieces (home invasion, warehouse, final house) feel like standard video game levels rather than specific, memorable sequences a human would obsess over. - **Voice concerns**: The intention mentions "short, punchy sentences during action" but this is often something LLMs say they will do while still producing somewhat formal prose. True pulp voice is harder for models to maintain consistently. - **The "hidden cache in her car" beat**: This is a particular LLM tell — giving the character convenient tools exactly when needed rather than forcing her to improvise throughout. The plan is generally solid in scope but feels too carefully engineered. It lacks the slight messiness, questionable choices, and specific obsessions that make a human-written revenge story feel alive. # Brainstorming Ideas to Make It More Human To counteract LLM tendencies, here are various approaches a human writer might take: - **Messier backstory**: Instead of ex-Marine Force Recon, make Riley an Army brat who learned to fight from her abusive father, or someone who spent time in juvenile detention, or a former underground fighter. Something that makes her skills feel more personal and less "official training." - **Uneven chapter focus**: Let Chapter 4 be almost entirely the desperate defense with Lucas (very human and tense), while Chapter 6 becomes a strange, mean detour where Riley does something genuinely disturbing to get information. - **Inconsistent morality**: Allow Riley to enjoy the violence more than she should. Let her scare Lucas at certain moments. Make her refuse to call the police not for noble reasons but because she wants to hurt people. - **Specific, weird details**: Humans obsess over particular images. Maybe Riley has a nervous habit of cracking her knuckles before fighting. Maybe she sings a specific stupid song to Lucas to keep him calm. Maybe one of the killers has an annoying laugh that haunts her. - **Naturalistic dialogue**: The antagonists should sound like real criminals, not movie thugs. The parents should be pathetic and human when facing consequences rather than cartoonishly evil. - **Sensory, specific violence**: Instead of generic "brutal action," focus on particular horrible details — the sound a knee makes when destroyed, the way blood looks on a specific carpet, the smell of gunpowder in a small bathroom. - **Imperfect ending**: Rather than disappearing "at peace," maybe Riley is actively struggling with what she's become. Maybe Lucas asks her not to leave. Maybe she keeps one of the killers' fingers as a souvenir. Something that feels unresolved. - **Voice variation**: Let the prose get sloppier and more primal as the story progresses. Start with relatively clean writing and let it degrade as Riley does. - **Unexpected tenderness**: Instead of consistent warmth with Lucas, maybe there are moments where she shows him genuine, clumsy care in the middle of chaos — trying to make him peanut butter toast during a lull in the fighting, or reading him a story while hiding in a closet. - **Personal obsessions**: A human writer might fixate on particular elements — the way suburban houses feel like traps, the particular fears of being a young woman who people assume is weak, the specific rage of being paid minimum wage to protect someone's child while they do criminal shit. - **Physical toll**: Let Riley get genuinely fucked up. By the final chapter she should be limping, bleeding, possibly concussed. The plan currently has her "wounded" in chapter 4 but doesn't make it bad enough. - **Wrong decisions**: Let her make some tactical errors that cost lives or make things harder. Perfect competence feels artificial. - **Cultural specificity**: Ground the story in a particular place and time. The house has specific ugly McMansion decor. The criminals have particular ethnic or regional speech patterns. The weapons and tactics feel like they come from somewhere specific rather than generic Hollywood fighting. These humanizing elements would help the story feel like it came from a specific person's obsessions and experiences rather than a well-crafted but somewhat generic pulp template. The goal is to make the reader feel the rage, the fear, the satisfaction, and the cost in a way that feels authentic rather than algorithmically satisfying.
Ok now with these considerations in mind, formulate the final plan for the a humanlike, compelling short piece in {n_chapters} chapters. Bear in mind the constraints of the piece (each chapter is just 1000 words). Above all things, the plan must serve the original prompt. We will use the same format as before: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention The story will be a raw, nasty, cathartic pulp revenge tale that fully honors the prompt: "They fucked with the wrong babysitter." The core fantasy is the electric satisfaction of an unassuming young woman who looks like the most harmless person imaginable turning into something terrifying when the people who dismissed her threaten a child in her care. Riley Kane is 25, small, quiet, and unremarkable-looking. She is not ex-special forces. She grew up rough — an Army brat with a violent father who taught her how to fight "so no one would ever put hands on her." She spent two years in juvenile detention as a teenager for nearly beating a boy to death. She’s been trying to stay clean for six years, working as a babysitter because it’s calm and no one expects anything from her. This job was supposed to be her fresh start. The tone is gritty and ugly when it needs to be, with bursts of dark, mean humor that feel like adrenaline talking. The violence will be specific, physical, and personal — never cool or cinematic for its own sake. Prose will favor short, jagged sentences during action and plain, almost flat language in the quiet parts. Voice will be third-person limited, tightly locked to Riley’s perspective, with occasional moments where her thinking becomes fragmented and primal. Key choices: Lucas (age 7) remains central the entire story. Riley never abandons him or drops him off somewhere safe for long stretches — protecting him is the engine. The parents are selfish mid-level criminals who aren’t cartoon evil; they’re pathetic and weak when it matters. The antagonists are believable, cruel, and constantly surprised by her. Riley’s rage escalates messily; she begins to enjoy it more than she should, and Lucas sees the change. The ending will not tie everything up with a bow. She protects the boy but is clearly damaged and darker by the end. Avoidances: No hidden weapon caches, no perfect military tactics, no long flashbacks, no neat moral justifications, no final redemption monologue. No chapter will feel like a video game level. Each 1000-word chapter must stand alone as a distinct movement while feeding the growing revenge. The plan deliberately leaves some chapters heavier on character and dread, others heavier on violence, to feel more human and less symmetrical. The story begins as a home invasion thriller and gradually mutates into a bloody revenge rampage that starts in the house and spills outward, always circling back to the child. Above all, the piece must feel like it comes from a specific, slightly unhinged human obsession with the moment when someone gentle snaps and decides the only answer left is to make them regret it. # Chapter Planning **Chapter 1: Ordinary Tuesday** Riley arrives at the big suburban house for her regular gig. We see her quiet dynamic with Lucas, the way the parents treat her like furniture, and their obvious nervousness before they leave for a “meeting.” Riley and Lucas settle in with pizza and cartoons. The chapter ends with the first unnerving phone call and Riley noticing a strange car parked across the street. (Focus: establishing normalcy, character, and the first low hum of dread.) **Chapter 2: Something Feels Wrong** Riley’s instincts are screaming. Lucas accidentally lets slip that his dad said “some bad men might come looking for him.” Power goes out. Riley arms herself with kitchen knives and a hammer while trying not to scare the boy. Brief, fragmented memories of her father and juvie surface only as sense memory, not exposition. The first intruder breaks a window. Chapter ends with the sickening realization that they are here for the child. **Chapter 3: They Touched Him** The home invasion begins in earnest. Three men kick the door in. One of them grabs Lucas. Riley kills the first man with the hammer in a sudden, ugly, panicked explosion of violence. The shock on the intruders’ faces when this tiny babysitter drops one of their guys is the turning point. She gets Lucas upstairs but is now bleeding from a knife wound. The men start taunting her through the walls, still not taking her seriously. **Chapter 4: This House Is Ours Tonight** Claustrophobic nightmare fuel. Riley and Lucas trapped on the second floor. She uses every trick she learned the hard way — broken glass on the stairs, a scalding trap, a choke wire made from a lamp cord. One deeply disturbing scene where she has to sing to Lucas to keep him quiet while she kills the second intruder in the hallway. By the end of the chapter she has decided: they don’t get to leave this house alive. **Chapter 5: No More Hiding** Riley realizes more men are coming. In a raw, ugly moment she tells Lucas the truth: “They fucked with the wrong babysitter.” She takes the fight downstairs, uses the dead men’s guns, and executes a brutal, messy escape from the house while carrying Lucas. The chapter is heavy on physical pain and her growing coldness. They steal one of the invaders’ cars and drive into the night with her bleeding all over the seat. **Chapter 6: Following the Thread** Now on the run with Lucas in the back seat, Riley starts hunting instead of hiding. She forces a wounded survivor to give up the name of the man who sent them. The interrogation is cruel and personal. Lucas watches her do it and becomes quieter, more afraid of her than of the bad men. First real glimpse of how far she’s willing to go. They track the crew to a low-rent strip-mall office where the rest are waiting for news. **Chapter 7: The Parents Get What They Deserve** Riley crashes the parents’ emergency rendezvous at a shitty motel. The parents are shocked to see her alive and covered in blood with their son. Confrontation is ugly — blame, excuses, pathetic begging. Riley makes the father call his boss and set up a meeting, using both parents as leverage. Lucas asks her to stop hurting people. She lies and says she will. The chapter ends with them driving to the final location, Riley’s hands shaking on the wheel. **Chapter 8: The Last Man** Final bloody confrontation at an abandoned self-storage facility where the boss is waiting. Riley walks into the trap deliberately, using the parents as distraction. A vicious, personal fight with the man who ordered the hit on the child. In the end she protects Lucas but crosses a final line that costs her something inside. The story closes on Riley handing Lucas over to arriving police with evidence against the entire ring, then walking away alone into the dark, changed and unable to go back to who she was. No neat conclusion, just the echo of the prompt fulfilled.
Perfect. Now with the outline more crystallised, and bearing in mind the discussion on human writing vs LLM pitfalls, we will flesh out our characters. Lets go through each of our main characters: - Write about their background, personality, idiosyncracies, flaws. Be specific and come up with examples to anchor & ground the character's profile (both core and trivial) - Briefly describe their physicality: appearance, how they carry themselves, express, interact with the world. - Concisely detail their motives, allegiences and existing relationships. Think from the perspective of the character as a real breathing thinking feeling individual in this world. - Write a couple quotes of flavour dialogue / internal monologue from the character to experiment with their voice. Output like this: # Character 1 name <character exploration> # Character 2 name <character exploration> etc
Model Output:
# Riley Kane **Background, personality, idiosyncrasies, flaws** Riley is 25. Grew up as an Army brat, constantly moving bases. Her father was a drill instructor with a hair-trigger temper who taught her how to fight “because the world eats weak girls.” When she was 15 she nearly beat a boy to death after he tried to drag her behind the bleachers; she did six months in juvenile detention. That experience scared her straight — or at least made her try. She’s been working as a babysitter for the last three years because it’s quiet, no one expects her to be anything, and she genuinely likes kids. She still has nightmares about the boy she almost killed and about her father’s voice in her head. Personality: Quiet, watchful, dryly funny when she’s comfortable. She has a habit of cracking the knuckles on her right hand when she’s stressed. She apologizes too much for small things. Flaws: She can shut down emotionally and become ice-cold once the switch flips. She has almost no self-worth outside of being “useful.” She sometimes enjoys hurting people more than she should, which terrifies her afterward. **Physicality** 5'3", small-boned, looks like a college sophomore who might teach piano. Shoulder-length brown hair she usually wears in a messy bun. Big, plain hazel eyes that make her look younger and softer than she is. She moves with deceptive stillness — economical, never wasting motion. When she’s relaxed she slouches; when danger appears she becomes eerily upright and balanced, like a coiled spring. She dresses in oversized hoodies and jeans so people underestimate her. Her hands are scarred across the knuckles. **Motives, allegiances, relationships** She took the job with the Millers because the pay was decent and Lucas is a good kid. She genuinely cares about him more than she wants to admit. She has no allegiance to the parents — she thinks they’re shitty, selfish people. Her only real relationship is with Lucas; she sees a bit of her younger self in him and wants to protect that softness. Deep down she’s lonely as hell and half expects everyone to eventually leave or betray her. **Voice** “I’m sorry, I know I’m just the babysitter, but if you ever put your hands on that boy again I will break every finger you’ve got.” *Internal monologue during the invasion:* “Okay. Okay. They touched him. That was the only rule. Everything else is paperwork now.” # Lucas Miller **Background, personality, idiosyncrasies, flaws** Seven years old. Only child of wealthy but neglectful parents. He’s bright, sensitive, and already understands that his mom and dad are scared of something. He has a stuffed dinosaur named Rex that he carries everywhere. He’s developed a habit of lying very still and pretending to be invisible when adults are fighting. He idolizes Riley because she actually listens to him and doesn’t treat him like an inconvenience. Personality: Sweet, curious, anxious. He asks a lot of “why” questions. When he’s scared he goes very quiet instead of crying. Flaw: He’s starting to believe that everyone who is nice to him eventually leaves or turns scary (a belief reinforced during this night). **Physicality** Small for his age, messy dark hair that falls in his eyes, pale skin. He carries himself curled inward, shoulders up around his ears. When he’s comfortable he bounces on his toes. He has a slight lisp on his S sounds that he’s self-conscious about. **Motives, allegiances, relationships** He just wants everyone to stop being mad. He loves Riley more than his actual parents at this point. He’s terrified of the bad men but even more terrified of what Riley becomes when she protects him. His only real allegiance is to Rex the dinosaur and to “Miss Riley.” **Voice** “Miss Riley… your hands are all red. Are you still you?” “Why do the monsters only come when Mommy and Daddy aren’t home?” # Derek Miller (the father) **Background, personality, idiosyncrasies, flaws** 38. Mid-level drug-and-extortion operator who fancies himself a businessman. Came from money and pissed most of it away on bad deals. He’s the one who actually owes the crew money. He talks a big game but folds instantly under real pressure. Collects expensive watches he can’t afford. Has a gambling problem he hides from his wife. Personality: Charming when he needs to be, whiny and pathetic when cornered. He calls everyone “buddy” or “pal.” Flaws: Cowardice masquerading as pragmatism. He will sacrifice anyone to save himself, including his own son. **Physicality** Soft around the middle, expensive haircut that’s starting to look ridiculous. Dresses like he wants people to think he’s in finance. Sweats visibly when nervous. Has a habit of checking his watch every thirty seconds when stressed. **Motives, allegiances, relationships** Loyal only to himself. He’s terrified of his wife finding out how badly he fucked up.
