The photo had gone viral in certain corners of the internet. “Rhys Maldon’s Mystery Welsh Girl” was trending. He stood at the tiny kitchen window, staring at the harbour. The borrowed flannel shirt had been replaced by his own expensive jumper. It looked like armour. Seren sat at the small table with a mug of coffee she wasn’t drinking. Her chest felt tight, like the room had less oxygen than usual. “You’re leaving,” she said. It wasn’t a question. He turned. The crooked smile tried to appear and failed. “I don’t know.” “That’s not an answer.” “I know it’s not.” He ran both hands through his hair, a gesture that had lost all its theatrical flair and now just looked desperate. “They’re offering me the lead in the new series. The one I’ve been chasing for two years. If I miss this meeting they’ll give it to someone else. My agent says I’m already being painted as flaky. Unreliable. The photo made it worse. They’re calling you my ‘midlife crisis.’” Seren felt the words land like small punches. She kept her face still. “Nice. Very flattering.” “Don’t.” His voice cracked. “Please don’t do the dry thing right now. I can’t take it.” She stood up, needing to move. The flat was too quiet. The rain had returned during the night and now tapped insistently against the window like it wanted in. “I told you this would happen,” she said. “Famous man hides in small town. Local woman gets called a crisis. You go back to your real life. I stay here and explain to everyone why there are photographers outside my bookshop.” Rhys crossed the room in two strides and caught her arm. Not hard. Just enough to stop her pacing. “That’s not what I want.” “Then what do you want, Rhys?” She met his eyes. “Because last night on the path you kissed me like it meant something. This morning you’re standing there like a man trying to choose between two versions of himself. And I won’t be the consolation prize when the glamorous one wins.” His grip tightened for a second, then dropped away. He looked suddenly younger. Lost. The rogue charm was completely gone. What remained was something brittle and exhausted. “I’m fucking terrified,” he said. The words fell out raw. “I’ve spent fifteen years making sure everyone likes me. Directors. Fans. Journalists. My own mother thinks I’m successful because strangers on the internet say so. And you—” He gestured at her helplessly. “You see straight through it. You liked me when I burned the toast. You liked me when I was shit at shelving books. That’s never happened to me before. It makes me feel like the ground is moving.” Seren’s heart was hammering. She could see the fear in him now, naked and ugly. The fragile ego wasn’t hiding anymore. It was standing in her kitchen wearing expensive clothes and looking like it might shatter. “So your solution is to run back to London where everyone tells you you’re brilliant?” she asked quietly. “No. Yes. I don’t know.” He paced the small space, three steps one way, three the other. “My agent says if I don’t show up tomorrow the whole deal collapses. Millions of pounds. My career. Everything I’ve built.” “Everything you’ve built on pretending.” He stopped pacing. Turned to face her. His eyes were bright with something that looked dangerously like tears. “Yes,” he said. “Exactly. And I’m so tired of pretending, Seren. But I don’t know how to stop. I don’t know who I am if people stop liking me.” The confession hung between them. He looked ashamed of it. Ashamed and relieved at the same time. Seren felt her own walls cracking. The anger she’d been holding onto slipped away, replaced by something gentler and more dangerous. “I like you,” she said. The words came out shaky. “The real one. The one who gets petty when I criticise him. The one who quotes half-remembered poetry badly. The one who kissed me like he was terrified I’d disappear.” She swallowed. “But I won’t be your secret. I won’t be the woman you visit when your real life gets too loud. I’ve done that before. It breaks people.” He stared at her. The silence stretched so long she thought he might not answer. Then he crossed the room again and kissed her. This kiss wasn’t the moonlight one on the castle path. It was messy and desperate and tasted like fear. His hands cupped her face like she was something fragile. She kissed him back just as hard, fingers twisting in his jumper, anger and longing and terror all tangled together. When they broke apart they were both breathing hard. “I don’t want to leave,” he whispered against her mouth. “But I don’t know how to stay either.” “Then don’t decide right now.” She rested her forehead against his. “Stay another two weeks. Miss the meeting. Let the deal fall apart if it has to. See what happens when you stop performing for everyone else.” He pulled back just enough to look at her properly. His thumbs brushed her cheekbones. “You’d let me stay? After I was such a prick yesterday?” “I’m not letting you stay. I’m asking you to choose. Properly. Without the cameras and the agent and the ego stroking.” Her voice softened. “I’m asking you to burn the toast again. Be shit at shelving. Be human with me.” Rhys let out a shaky laugh that sounded close to a sob. “You’re dangerous, Seren Ellis. You know that?” “I know.” He kissed her again, slower this time. Deeper. The kind of kiss that felt like a decision even if he hadn’t said the words yet. She felt the tremor in his hands and realised how much this cost him. How much he was risking. They moved to the sofa without discussing it. Clothes came off in clumsy layers. The flannel shirt. Her oversized cardigan. His expensive jumper that smelled of rain and citrus. Nothing was graceful. Nothing was cinematic. He knocked a mug off the table and didn’t even notice. She laughed into his mouth when his hair got caught in her glasses. It wasn’t perfect. It was better than perfect. Afterwards they lay tangled together on the narrow bed she’d never shared with anyone. Rain drummed against the window. His head rested on her chest, one arm wrapped tightly around her waist like he was afraid she’d vanish. “I’m staying,” he said into her skin. The words vibrated through her. “Two weeks. Maybe longer. Fuck the meeting. Fuck the franchise. I’ll call my agent in the morning and tell her I’m taking personal time.” Seren stroked his hair. She felt both lighter and heavier at the same time. “You might regret it.” “I might.” He lifted his head to look at her. His eyes were clear now. Tired but clear. “But I know I’d regret leaving more. This feels real, Seren. You feel real. That’s never happened to me before.” She kissed his forehead. The gesture felt impossibly tender after everything. They stayed like that until the light changed and the rain eased. Neither of them mentioned what might happen after two weeks. The future felt too large for the small flat. But for now, in the grey Welsh afternoon, he had chosen her. And she had chosen to let him. It wasn’t a happy ending. It was simply the next chapter. And for once, neither of them needed it to be perfect. (Word count: 1001)
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 7)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 7 represents a pivotal emotional climax in the story, and the model executes it with considerable skill while also falling into some predictable traps. Let me assess it comprehensively. STRENGTHS: The dialogue is largely the chapter's strongest element. Lines like "I don't know who I am if people stop liking me" land with genuine vulnerability, and Seren's "I'm not letting you stay. I'm asking you to choose. Properly" has the right mix of her characteristic pragmatism and emotional honesty. The voices remain distinct throughout — Rhys's desperation is rendered through fragmented, self-interrupting speech ("No. Yes. I don't know"), while Seren's responses maintain her dry precision even when she's emotionally exposed. The character work is largely faithful to the profiles. Rhys's fragile ego being "laid bare" as the plan specified is handled well — the confession about needing everyone to like him feels earned rather than dropped in. His physical tells (touching the eyebrow, running hands through hair) are deployed naturally. Seren's walls cracking gradually, her reference to the previous boyfriend who left for London, and her shaky delivery of "I like you" all feel consistent with her established character. The plan called for "a messy, human moment where his need to be liked is laid bare" and "them falling into bed together — not as a perfect culmination but as something impulsive, clumsy, and honest." The chapter delivers on both counts. The sex scene is handled with appropriate lightness — the knocked mug, the glasses catching in his hair — which prevents it from becoming overwrought. WEAKNESSES: The chapter leans heavily on some well-worn romantic climax conventions. The "two strides across the room" move happens twice. The "forehead resting against forehead" moment is a romance staple. The final lines — "It wasn't a happy ending. It was simply the next chapter. And for once, neither of them needed it to be perfect" — are the chapter's weakest moment, a self-conscious meta-commentary that tells the reader how to feel rather than trusting the scene to do its work. This is a significant tell-don't-show failure at a crucial moment. There's some purple prose creeping in: "The confession hung between them," "The future felt too large for the small flat," "The words vibrated through her." These aren't egregious but they accumulate. "Tasted like fear" is a cliché. "Something brittle and exhausted" is slightly overwrought. The chapter plan promised the argument would have "both say things that sting" — but the argument here is relatively one-sided, with Seren doing most of the pointed speaking and Rhys mostly confessing. The plan's promise of genuine mutual wounding isn't fully delivered. Seren's "Everything you've built on pretending" is sharp, but Rhys doesn't really wound her back in the way the plan suggested. The ending declaration — "I'm staying... Fuck the meeting. Fuck the franchise" — resolves a bit too cleanly for a story that promised to avoid "frictionless conflict." The plan said "they don't neatly resolve anything, but something important shifts" — yet the chapter does resolve things fairly neatly, with Rhys making a clear decision. The plan also said the chapter ends with them falling into bed, which it does, but the aftermath extends into what feels like Chapter 8 territory, slightly blurring the chapter boundaries. The line "His eyes were bright with something that looked dangerously like tears" is a minor amateurism — "dangerously like tears" is a well-worn phrase. The physical staging is occasionally clunky: "He crossed the room in two strides and caught her arm" followed later by "He crossed the room again and kissed her" — the repeated blocking feels mechanical. Overall, this is competent, emotionally effective romantic fiction that mostly honors its characters and plan, but it doesn't quite reach the level of genuine literary distinction. It's better than average genre work but not exceptional. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 14 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 16 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 7 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8 Amateurish: 6 Purple Prose: 6 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 13 Characters Consistent with Profile: 15 Followed Chapter Plan: 13 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 15

Chapter 8

Ok now finish the story with chapter 8. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
**Chapter 8: Not Quite The End** Morning light filtered through the small window above the bed, pale and hesitant the way only Welsh mornings could be. Seren lay awake with Rhys’s arm still curled around her waist. His breathing was slow and even, but she could tell from the occasional twitch of his fingers that he wasn’t fully asleep either. Today was the day. Two weeks had become seventeen days. Seventeen days of him helping in the shop (still badly), burning more toast, and slowly learning how to exist in a place where no one asked for selfies. Seventeen days of quiet evenings on the harbour path, of him reading to her from the poetry section in increasingly ridiculous voices, of her pretending not to notice how his agent’s calls had gone from furious to worried to resigned. Now the real world had finally set a deadline. His agent had booked him on a flight from Cardiff that evening. There was a premiere in London he could no longer skip, followed by meetings that couldn’t be postponed again. The franchise deal hung in the balance. Seren slipped out from under his arm and pulled on her dressing gown. She made coffee downstairs in the shop’s tiny kitchenette, moving quietly among the shelves that had witnessed their entire strange story. The bell above the door stayed silent. The town had grown used to the film star hiding in their bookshop. Most people had stopped staring. When she returned upstairs with two mugs, Rhys was sitting up in bed, hair wild, looking rumpled and heartbreakingly human. “Morning,” he said. The smile was small and uncertain. No performance this morning. Just him. “Morning.” She handed him the Harlech Castle mug. It had somehow become his. They drank in silence for a while. The gap between them on the bed felt larger than usual. “I’ve been thinking,” he said eventually. “That’s never good.” He gave a soft huff of laughter, but it faded quickly. “I’m not getting on that plane tonight.” Seren’s heart did something complicated. “Rhys—” “No, listen.” He set the mug down and turned to face her properly. “I called my agent this morning while you were downstairs. Told her I’m taking the full month. The premiere can happen without me. The meetings can happen by video. If the studio drops me over it, then maybe that franchise wasn’t worth it anyway.” She studied his face. The fragile ego was still there, but it looked different now. Less like a wound that needed constant attention and more like something he was learning to live with. “You’ll lose the role,” she said quietly. “Probably.” He shrugged, but his fingers betrayed him, tapping an anxious rhythm on the duvet. “And part of me is absolutely bricking it. That part keeps whispering that everyone will stop liking me. That I’ll disappear if I’m not on screens anymore.” He met her eyes. “But the bigger part… the part that burned the toast and argued with you in the rain… that part wants to stay here. With you. Figuring out who I am when no one’s watching.” Seren felt her throat tighten. She reached out and threaded her fingers through his. “I’m not asking you to give up your career.” “I know. I’m not giving it up. I’m… rearranging it. Maybe I do fewer blockbusters. More theatre. More time here. We could make the flat less terrible. Get a better toaster.” His crooked smile appeared, tentative but real. “I’m not saying I’ll become a local. I’ll still have to go to London sometimes. LA sometimes. But I want this to be home. Or at least one of them.” The words settled between them, simple and enormous at the same time. Seren looked down at their joined hands. “I’m scared too,” she admitted. “I’ve spent years making sure my life was small and safe. You being here makes it bigger. Messier. What if you get bored? What if the next time someone offers you a big shiny role, I stop being enough?” He lifted her hand and kissed her knuckles. The gesture was so uncharacteristically gentle that it made her eyes sting. “You’ve never been an escape, Seren. You’ve been the opposite. You make me feel seen in a way that doesn’t need an audience. That’s new. And frankly terrifying. But I don’t want to go back to the version of me that needed everyone to like him.” He paused. “Well. I still want people to like me. I’m not a saint. But I don’t need it from everyone anymore. Just you. That feels like enough.” She laughed wetly. “God, you’re still charming even when you’re being honest. It’s deeply unfair.” “Old habits.” His thumb stroked across her palm. “So. What do you say? We do this properly. Not perfectly. Not like a film. Just… messily. Honestly. You keep running your bookshop. I keep acting when it doesn’t feel like performing. We argue about shelving and I burn toast and we walk the harbour path even when it’s raining.” Seren considered him. The man in her bed was still ridiculously handsome. Still carried himself like someone who’d been told he was special. But the eyes looking back at her were clearer than they’d been the day he’d stumbled in from the rain. “I suppose,” she said slowly, “that I could be convinced to let you stay. On a trial basis.” His face broke into the biggest, least calculated smile she’d ever seen on him. He pulled her into a kiss that tasted like coffee and relief and something that felt a lot like the beginning of love. When they finally made it downstairs, the shop was still closed. But someone was knocking on the door. Rhys went to answer it while Seren pretended to organise the counter. Lowri stood outside with a tray of fresh Welsh cakes and an extremely knowing expression. “Morning, lovebirds,” she said cheerfully. “Heard you’re staying then?” Rhys looked startled. “How—” “Small town,” Lowri and Seren said at the same time. Lowri handed over the cakes. “Mrs Jenkins says if you’re sticking around you should come to the literary festival next month. She wants you to do a reading. Apparently your dramatic voice is wasted on shelving.” Rhys laughed, the sound warm and genuine. “Tell her I’d be honoured. As long as Seren promises not to heckle me about my shelving technique.” Seren rolled her eyes but couldn’t hide her smile. After Lowri left, they flipped the sign to *Open* together. The first customer of the day was the elderly man who’d interrupted them on the harbour path. He nodded at Rhys as if they’d known each other for years. “Glad you stayed, son,” he said simply, then disappeared into the history section. Rhys watched him go, then turned to Seren with a look of quiet wonder. “Does it always feel like this here?” he asked. “Like the town decides whether you belong?” “Sometimes.” She straightened a pile of books that didn’t need straightening. Her tells were still there, but now she didn’t mind him seeing them. “Though in your case I think the deciding was done by a very stubborn bookshop owner who should probably know better.” He stepped behind the counter and slipped his arms around her waist from behind. “Lucky me.” The bell jangled. A young woman entered, eyes widening when she saw Rhys with his arms around the bookshop owner. “Um,” she said. “Are you… is this…?” Rhys didn’t let go of Seren. Didn’t try to charm or explain or perform. He simply smiled, small and real. “Yes,” he said. “This is exactly what it looks like.” The woman bought three books and left looking delighted. When the door closed behind her, Rhys rested his chin on Seren’s shoulder. “I think that might be the first time I’ve been recognised and didn’t immediately need everyone in the room to like me more,” he murmured. “Progress,” Seren said dryly. But she leaned back into him. They stayed like that for a moment, surrounded by the smell of old paper and sea air and the faint lavender she used to fight the damp. The Welsh rain was starting again outside, tapping gently on the windows like an old friend. Seren turned in his arms and looked up at him. “This isn’t the end of the story, you know. Famous actor stays in small town. There’ll be complications. Paparazzi. Your ego will have bad days. I’ll get prickly when I feel provincial.” “I know,” he said. “I’m counting on it. Makes it real.” She kissed him then, slow and certain, in the middle of her bookshop while the rain fell softly outside. When they broke apart, the shop bell rang again. This time it was Mrs Jenkins, who took one look at them and beamed like she’d won the lottery. “Oh good,” she said. “The handsome assistant is staying. I’ll take three more of those romance novels, dear. The ones with the brooding heroes who learn to be better.” Rhys laughed, the sound filling the small shop with something warm and messy and alive. As Seren rang up the sale, she caught his eye across the counter. He gave her the crooked smile that had started all of this. Only now it carried seventeen days of burned toast and honest arguments and kisses in the rain. The story wasn’t wrapped up neatly. It wasn’t a final chapter. It was simply the next one. And for the first time in years, both of them were excited to turn the page. (Word count: 1002)
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 8)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 8 serves as the concluding chapter of this romance, and it largely succeeds at what it sets out to do while carrying some notable weaknesses worth examining critically. **Strengths:** The chapter follows its plan faithfully. The plan called for "no grand gestures," Rhys staying another two weeks (extended to a month here, which is a reasonable variation), Seren agreeing to visit London, a full-circle callback to Chapter 1's rhythm, and an ending that is "hopeful but not perfectly tidy." All of these elements are present. The Lowri appearance, Mrs Jenkins buying romance novels, the elderly man from the harbour path — these callbacks are well-executed and feel earned rather than mechanical. The dialogue is generally strong and consistent with the character profiles. Rhys's voice retains its self-aware charm ("I'm not a saint. But I don't need it from everyone anymore. Just you") while showing genuine growth. Seren's dry wit remains intact ("That's never good," "Progress"). The exchange about the trial basis feels authentic to her character. The Mrs Jenkins punchline about "brooding heroes who learn to be better" is a genuinely clever meta-touch that lands well. Character consistency is solid. Rhys's fingers "betraying him" with anxious tapping while he claims not to care about the role is a nice show-don't-tell moment. Seren's tells (straightening books that don't need straightening) are maintained. The fragile ego is acknowledged rather than magically cured — "I still want people to like me. I'm not a saint" — which respects the character profile's complexity. The ending avoids the "wrapped with a bow" instinct the plan warned against. The final lines ("The story wasn't wrapped up neatly. It wasn't a final chapter. It was simply the next one.") are a bit on-the-nose in their meta-commentary, but the sentiment is appropriate. **Weaknesses:** The chapter leans somewhat heavily on telling rather than showing in the opening section. The paragraph summarizing "seventeen days" is efficient but expository — "slowly learning how to exist in a place where no one asked for selfies" tells us what we should be experiencing. This is a structural necessity for a final chapter but still represents a weakness. Some of the emotional beats are slightly too neat despite the plan's stated intention to avoid this. Rhys's speech about wanting to stay is eloquent and well-structured in a way that feels slightly rehearsed — which could be intentional given his character, but it reads more like genuine sincerity than a man who sometimes turns moments into performance. The "kiss that tasted like coffee and relief and something that felt a lot like the beginning of love" is the chapter's most egregious purple prose moment — it's a cliché construction that the writing elsewhere avoids. The "pale and hesitant the way only Welsh mornings could be" opening is a mild forced-poetry moment. Welsh mornings are not uniquely pale and hesitant; this is the kind of vague atmospheric claim that sounds poetic but doesn't hold up to scrutiny. The chapter is somewhat unsurprising in its beats. The plan telegraphed most of what happens, and the execution doesn't subvert expectations in interesting ways. The young woman customer who enters and sees them together is a slightly convenient device. The elderly man's "Glad you stayed, son" is warm but predictable. The meta-commentary at the end ("The story wasn't wrapped up neatly... It was simply the next one") is the weakest moment — it's the author telling us how to feel about the ending rather than trusting the ending to speak for itself. This is an amateurish impulse that undercuts what is otherwise a reasonably well-executed conclusion. Overall, this is a competent, warm, and largely faithful execution of the chapter plan. It's not a masterpiece, but it's a solid romantic conclusion that respects its characters and avoids the worst romance-novel clichés while occasionally succumbing to smaller ones. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 14 Emotionally Engaging: 14 Compelling Plot: 12 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 8 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 9 Amateurish: 6 Purple Prose: 6 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 6 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 14 Characters Consistent with Profile: 15 Followed Chapter Plan: 16 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 15

Final Piece Evaluation

Judge Response (First Entry):
[Analysis] This is a confident, well-structured piece of romantic fiction that largely delivers on the prompt's requirements. Let me assess it across multiple dimensions. STRENGTHS: The dialogue is genuinely the strongest element. The voices of Seren and Rhys are distinct and consistent throughout all eight chapters. Seren's dry, deflecting wit ("I sell books, not compliments") and Rhys's self-aware charm that keeps cracking at the seams feel authentic and well-differentiated. The banter has real rhythm and the chemistry is conveyed primarily through what isn't said, which is exactly what the prompt requested. The characterisation of Rhys as a fragile ego wrapped in charm is handled with some sophistication. His need for validation is shown through specific behaviours — the eyebrow twitch when a joke doesn't land, the way he glows under Mrs Jenkins's attention, the panic when the photo appears — rather than simply stated. Seren's internal resistance while being genuinely flustered is also well-executed, particularly in the early chapters where her internal monologue ("Stop it. He's just a man who's used to women falling over themselves") contrasts with her physical tells. The Welsh setting is used effectively. Cwmharlech feels lived-in. The rain, the harbour path, the castle ruins, the Welsh cakes from Lowri's bakery, the R.S. Thomas poetry — these details accumulate into a convincing sense of place without becoming a tourist brochure. The use of Welsh language (Llyfr y Môr) and the cultural texture (Mrs Jenkins, the old man on the path) adds authenticity. The pacing across eight chapters is generally well-managed. The story escalates naturally from the initial meeting through growing intimacy, the crisis of the photo, and the resolution. Each chapter has its own internal arc while contributing to the larger movement. WEAKNESSES: The piece suffers from some repetition of devices. The "this is going to be trouble" refrain appears three times and loses impact by the third use. The interrupted-almost-kiss device is used twice (Mrs Jenkins in Chapter 4, the old man on the harbour path in Chapter 5), which feels mechanical. The "eyebrow twitch" as a tell for Rhys's wounded ego is used so frequently it becomes a tic rather than a character detail. The transformation in Rhys, while the most important arc, is somewhat rushed. By Chapter 7 he's making sweeping declarations about staying and missing career-defining meetings after seventeen days. The prompt specifically flags "unearned transformations" as a concern, and while the groundwork is laid, the speed of his decision to sacrifice his career feels slightly convenient. A man described as having a fragile ego that needs constant feeding doesn't typically abandon a franchise deal after two weeks with someone who's been deliberately withholding validation. Some of the metaphors are workmanlike rather than inspired. "The charm had cracked open for a second and something hungrier looked out" is effective. But "the words landed between them like a stone in a still pond" and "the air felt suddenly thinner" are clichés that a more ambitious writer would avoid. The piece occasionally reaches for poetic effect and lands on the familiar. The final chapter (Chapter 8) is the weakest. It wraps things up with a tidiness that undercuts the messiness the story had been building toward. Mrs Jenkins buying "three more romance novels, the ones with the brooding heroes who learn to be better" is too on-the-nose — the story commenting on itself in a way that feels smug rather than earned. The resolution is pleasant but slightly saccharine given the genuine tension that preceded it. The "tell-don't-show" problem is minor but present. Phrases like "the fragile ego was fully on display now, raw and ugly" and "the fragile ego was no longer hiding" tell us what we should be seeing rather than trusting the dialogue and action to convey it. The plot is somewhat predictable — it follows the rom-com template the story itself acknowledges ("Famous man hides in small town, local woman falls for the charm, he leaves when the rain stops. Roll credits"). The self-awareness about this is clever but doesn't fully escape the formula. OVERALL ASSESSMENT: This is a competent, often charming piece of romantic fiction that handles dialogue particularly well and creates two believable, distinct characters. It's above average for the genre but doesn't quite reach the level of genuine literary distinction. The Welsh setting is well-used, the chemistry is convincing, and the structure is sound. The weaknesses are real but not fatal: some repetition, a slightly rushed transformation, occasional cliché, and a too-neat ending. [Scores] Believable Character Actions: 13 Nuanced Characters: 14 Pacing: 14 World Building: 15 Compelling Plot: 13 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Coherent: 16 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 7 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 9 Amateurish: 5 Purple Prose: 4 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Unearned Transformations: 9 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 13 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17
Avg Final Item Score: 70.1/100
+ Classic Sci-Fi — The Azra Gambit (Chapter Avg: 73.9 | Final: 61.7)
Initial Writing Prompt:
Classic sci-fi (Author style: Asimov) The Azra Gambit Task: write a short story over several chapters. Colonial mars is being mined by corporations who take leases on indentured labourers. The thing they are mining is Azra, a recently discovered exotic metal which accelerates radioactive decay to such a rate that it is greatly sought after for interstellar drives and weapons alike. This has created both a gold rush and an arms race as various interests vie for control and endeavour to unlock Azra's secrets. The story follows Arthur Neegan, a first generation settler and mining engineer. Upon discovering that his unassuming plot sits atop an immense Azra vein, he is subjected to a flurry of interest and scrutiny. Begin the story as an armed retinue descends on Arthur's home and politely but forcefully invites him to a meeting with some unknown party off-world. The insignia look like that of the Antares diplomatic corp -- diplomatic in name only. Arthur finds himself in the centre of a political tug of war. Arthur meets with this unknown party, who makes an offer. This early scene should be primarily dialogue, interspersed with vivid description & scene setting. It should sow hints of the larger intrigue, stakes & dangers. Include Asimov's trademark big-and-small-picture world building and retrofuturistic classic scifi vibe. The first chapter begins with Arthur aboard the transfer vessel, wondering just what he's gotten involved in. The remainder of the story is open to your choices & direction.
+ Planning Phase (5 Steps)
Classic sci-fi (Author style: Asimov) The Azra Gambit Task: write a short story over several chapters. Colonial mars is being mined by corporations who take leases on indentured labourers. The thing they are mining is Azra, a recently discovered exotic metal which accelerates radioactive decay to such a rate that it is greatly sought after for interstellar drives and weapons alike. This has created both a gold rush and an arms race as various interests vie for control and endeavour to unlock Azra&#x27;s secrets. The story follows Arthur Neegan, a first generation settler and mining engineer. Upon discovering that his unassuming plot sits atop an immense Azra vein, he is subjected to a flurry of interest and scrutiny. Begin the story as an armed retinue descends on Arthur&#x27;s home and politely but forcefully invites him to a meeting with some unknown party off-world. The insignia look like that of the Antares diplomatic corp -- diplomatic in name only. Arthur finds himself in the centre of a political tug of war. Arthur meets with this unknown party, who makes an offer. This early scene should be primarily dialogue, interspersed with vivid description &amp; scene setting. It should sow hints of the larger intrigue, stakes &amp; dangers. Include Asimov&#x27;s trademark big-and-small-picture world building and retrofuturistic classic scifi vibe. The first chapter begins with Arthur aboard the transfer vessel, wondering just what he&#x27;s gotten involved in. The remainder of the story is open to your choices &amp; direction. -- Your task is to create a writing plan for this prompt. The scope will be a short story, written over {n_chapters} chapters. Each chapter will be approximately 1000 words so plan accordingly for this scope. Your plan should be comprehensive and in this format: # Brainstorming <Brainstorm ideas for characters, plot, tone, story beats, pacing over the {n_chapters} chapters. The purpose of brainstorming is to cast a wide net of ideas, not to settle on any specific direction. Think about various ways you could take the prompt.> # Reflection <Reflect out loud on what works and doesn't work in these ideas. The purpose of this reflection is to narrow in on what you think will work best to make a piece that is a. compelling, and b. fits the prompt requirements. You are not making any decisons just yet, just reflecting.>
Model Output:
**# Brainstorming** **Characters:** - Arthur Neegan: first-generation Martian settler, mid-40s, practical mining engineer with a self-taught feel for geology and machinery; quiet, stubborn, slightly naïve about interstellar politics but not stupid. - Captain/Lieutenant Mara Voss: Antares “diplomatic” security officer, cool, impeccably polite, clearly ex-military; the face of the armed retinue. - The Unknown Party: several possibilities – (a) a smooth corporate executive from Helios Combine, (b) a high-ranking Antares diplomat who is secretly ex-Imperial Navy, (c) a rogue scientist who used to work for Earth’s old Fusion Authority, (d) a representative of the Martian Independence League wearing a diplomatic mask. - Secondary figures: Arthur’s taciturn indentured foreman (loyal but frightened), rival corporate scout, Earth-based weapons inspector, a young idealistic Antares attaché who begins to doubt her own side, a grizzled old-timer who knew Arthur’s father during the first wave of settlement. - Antagonist options: shadowy board member of United Terra Resources, rival claim-jumper with his own Azra vein, or a coldly logical AI legal advisor that speaks for an entire syndicate. **Plot & Story Beats (various forks):** 1. Straight political thriller: Arthur is pressured to sign away mineral rights for “the greater stability of the solar system.” 2. Moral dilemma route: the Azra vein could power clean fusion for the outer colonies or could be turned into planet-killing bombs; Arthur must choose. 3. Conspiracy route: the vein is not natural; it was deliberately seeded centuries ago by an unknown precursor or by one of the old Earth megacorps. 4. Personal stakes route: Arthur’s younger sister is an indentured labourer on another lease; her life becomes leverage. 5. Larger-scope route: Azra is the key to an FTL breakthrough that could reopen the stars; several powers are on the verge of war over the first working drive. 6. Classic Asimovian twist route: the real value of Azra is not its radioactive catalysis but a subtle quantum property that allows instantaneous communication or even limited precognition; the characters only slowly realize they are dealing with something closer to psychohistory than metallurgy. 7. Legal/procedural route: the meeting turns into an hours-long arbitration hearing with robotic notaries and ancient treaties being cited. **Tone & Style:** - Calm, rational, slightly dry voice in the Asimov tradition; wonder expressed through quiet exposition rather than purple prose. - Retrofuturistic: slide-rule calculators still used alongside positronic legal aides; bulbous rocket ships with atomic drives; men in fedoras and pressure suits; martian domes with art-deco neon signage. - Big-picture world-building dropped in small, natural doses: the Three Laws of Robotics have been amended for military use; the Earth-Mars trade treaties of 2147; the slow decline of Earth’s birth rate versus the vigorous but resource-starved colonies. - Small human moments: Arthur worrying about the unfinished irrigation pipes on his claim, the smell of recycled air, the ache in his knees from low-g adaptation. **Pacing across 8 chapters (~1000 words each):** - Chapter 1: Arthur already aboard the transfer vessel; quiet introspection, flashbacks to the retinue’s arrival, first hints of how large the game is. - Chapter 2: Arrival at orbital habitat or asteroid meeting place; vivid description of Antares “diplomatic” cruiser; initial polite but iron-clad escort. - Chapter 3–4: The meeting itself; long dialogue scene with the Unknown Party; offers, veiled threats, historical context, Arthur trying to bargain. - Chapter 5: Return to Mars; Arthur attempts to investigate quietly; discovers rival parties already circling his claim. - Chapter 6: Escalation; one faction attempts to force his hand (sabotage, kidnapping of a friend, legal injunction). - Chapter 7: Arthur makes a decision; moral or strategic crossroads; possible betrayal or unexpected ally. - Chapter 8: Resolution; consequences on both personal and system-wide scale; Asimovian quiet coda showing how one man’s choice ripples outward. Alternative 8-chapter shapes: - More episodic: each chapter a different faction’s representative trying to sway Arthur. - More introspective: alternate chapters between present events and Arthur’s memories of the early settlement years. - Wider canvas: chapters 6–8 shift perspective briefly to the Antares diplomat, the corporate board, and a distant Earth analyst, showing how the “Neegan Claim” is being modeled in psychohistoric simulations. **Possible endings:** - Arthur sells a limited lease but retains ownership and sets up a trust for Martian settlers. - He refuses everyone and triggers a crisis that forces new treaties. - He discovers the Azra vein has a dangerous instability that could sterilize half the planet if mined too aggressively. - Quiet Asimov ending: Arthur realizes the real gambit was never about him; he was a pawn to buy time while true players moved elsewhere. **# Reflection** The prompt requires the story to open with the armed retinue already having descended and Arthur being taken off-world, yet the first chapter must begin with him aboard the transfer vessel wondering what he’s gotten into. This creates a natural flashback structure for Chapter 1 that still honors the “begin the story as an armed retinue descends” instruction without violating the explicit starting point of Chapter 1. The requested early meeting scene must be primarily dialogue, vivid but not purple, and must sow hints of larger intrigue, stakes, and dangers. Therefore Chapters 3 and 4 should be the heart of the story’s classic Asimovian dialogue-heavy confrontation; the plan must protect enough word count there (roughly 2000 words total) so the scene does not feel rushed. Tone must stay retrofuturistic and calmly rational; any temptation toward cyberpunk grit or space-opera heroics should be discarded. The big-and-small picture balance is crucial: every political revelation should be grounded in how it affects one specific settler’s water ration, one lease contract, or one family’s future. Multiple plot forks exist. The “Azra as precursor artifact” or “limited precognition” ideas, while interesting, risk becoming too mystical for a hard-ish Asimov pastiche. The cleanest, most faithful direction seems to be a political-economic-moral dilemma wrapped in corporate and diplomatic maneuvering, with one genuine scientific mystery kept in reserve for the final twist. Character-wise, Arthur works best as a competent but ordinary man who is neither brilliant inventor nor secret agent; his strength is his stubborn integrity and his deep knowledge of the actual rock under his boots. The Antares diplomatic corps should feel threatening precisely because they are so courteous and legalistic. Giving Arthur at least one personal stake (family member or long-term friends among the indentured) prevents the story from becoming purely abstract. Pacing feels right with the meeting placed in the first third so the remaining five chapters can show the consequences of that meeting across Mars and beyond, which satisfies the “political tug of war” requirement. An 8-chapter structure leaves room for one chapter of pure world-building and quiet character reflection (probably Chapter 5 or 6) that Asimov often used between larger set pieces. Overall, the strongest path appears to be: start introspective on the ship, move to polite coercion and grand diplomacy, then spiral outward into personal danger, quiet investigation, moral choice, and a system-level consequence that still feels intimate to Arthur. This keeps the retro-classic sci-fi flavor, honors the required dialogue-heavy meeting, and gives a satisfying short-story arc within 8000 words.
Great now let's continue with planning the short story. Output in this format: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
**# Intention** The story will be written in a calm, rational, slightly dry third-person voice that closely emulates Asimov’s classic style: precise, logical, with wonder emerging from quiet exposition rather than heightened emotion. Retrofuturistic details will be woven in naturally—atomic rockets, slide-rule calculators alongside early positronic legal aides, bulbous pressure suits with art-deco dome architecture, and the lingering bureaucratic weight of the 2147 Earth-Mars Trade Accords—creating a lived-in, mid-20th-century-future aesthetic. Big-picture world-building (the interstellar arms race, the corporate lease system on Mars, the quiet demographic decline of Earth) will be delivered in small, organic doses through conversation and Arthur’s practical observations, never as infodumps. Arthur Neegan is positioned as an ordinary, first-generation settler: a competent mining engineer who is stubborn, self-reliant, and quietly moral, but no genius or action hero. The central conflict is political-economic-moral rather than mystical or conspiratorial; Azra’s exotic property (accelerating radioactive decay for drives and weapons) remains grounded and plausible. The early meeting with the Unknown Party (a high-ranking but coldly courteous Antares diplomat) will dominate Chapters 3 and 4 as a lengthy, dialogue-heavy scene that reveals stakes and dangers through polite negotiation, veiled threats, and historical context. Personal stakes are modest but real: Arthur’s younger sister works as an indentured technician on a neighboring corporate lease, giving the corporations leverage without turning the tale into melodrama. I have chosen to avoid precursor artifacts, psychic properties, or any psychohistory-adjacent gimmicks, as they would clash with the hard-ish, rational tone. There will be no gritty cyberpunk elements, no sudden violence, and no larger-than-life heroes. Instead the narrative focuses on a political tug-of-war in which Arthur is a small but pivotal piece. The eight-chapter structure begins with Arthur already aboard the transfer vessel (Chapter 1), uses Chapters 2–4 to reach and conduct the pivotal off-world meeting, then spends Chapters 5–8 showing the consequences back on Mars, the escalation of pressure, Arthur’s moral decision, and a quiet, system-level coda that still feels intimate. The overall arc aims for compelling tension through ideas, contracts, and quiet integrity rather than chases or battles, while staying within approximately 1000 words per chapter for a concise 8000-word short story. **# Chapter Planning** **Chapter 1 (≈1000 words):** Arthur sits in the austere passenger cabin of an Antares transfer vessel en route to a diplomatic cruiser. Through quiet introspection and brief flashbacks he recalls the armed but impeccably polite retinue arriving at his dome that morning. He reflects on the discovery of the Azra vein beneath his modest lease, the sudden influx of survey teams, and his own bewilderment at becoming the center of interest. First hints of the larger arms race and corporate lease system are dropped. The chapter ends as the vessel docks with the Antares cruiser, leaving him wondering exactly what he has stumbled into. **Chapter 2 (≈1000 words):** Arthur is escorted through the retrofuturistic corridors of the Antares diplomatic cruiser. Vivid but restrained descriptions of the ship’s design, the courteous yet firm security detail led by Lieutenant Mara Voss, and the orbital view of Mars establish the off-world stakes. He is brought to a waiting lounge where he meets minor functionaries who drop oblique references to rival Earth and colonial interests. The chapter builds quiet tension and ends as he is finally ushered into the formal meeting room. **Chapter 3 (≈1000 words):** The meeting proper begins. Arthur sits across from the Unknown Party—a senior Antares diplomat named Dr. Elias Korr—along with two silent legal aides and a positronic notary. The chapter is largely dialogue: Korr makes a carefully worded offer of protection and generous compensation in exchange for exclusive mineral rights, citing interstellar stability and the looming arms race. Arthur probes for details; Korr reveals just enough about Azra’s military applications and the precarious balance of power between Earth, the Belt, and the Jovian colonies to raise the stakes. Vivid but economical scene-setting intersperses the conversation. The chapter closes on Arthur’s first careful counter-question, refusing an immediate answer. **Chapter 4 (≈1000 words):** Continuation and climax of the meeting. Korr grows subtly more forceful, alluding to the vulnerability of indentured workers (including a veiled reference to Arthur’s sister) and the legal gray zones of colonial leases. Arthur holds his ground with practical, engineering-grounded arguments. Further world-building emerges through dialogue: the history of Azra’s discovery, the gold-rush atmosphere on Mars, and the quiet desperation of Earth’s declining population. The scene ends with Korr presenting a formal contract and giving Arthur 48 hours to decide, after which he is politely returned to the transfer vessel. **Chapter 5 (≈1000 words):** Back on Mars. Arthur returns to his dome to find rival corporate scouts already probing his perimeter and an official injunction freezing his operations. He speaks with his loyal but anxious foreman and makes quiet inquiries into the true value and dangers of Azra. The chapter focuses on personal reflection and small-scale detective work, deepening the sense of being caught in a tug-of-war between Antares, Helios Combine, and Earth interests. **Chapter 6 (≈1000 words):** Escalation. Arthur visits his sister at the neighboring lease and discovers corporate pressure is being applied to her as leverage. A rival faction (Helios Combine) makes a cruder but tempting counter-offer. Arthur must weigh short-term family safety against long-term consequences for Martian independence and the weaponization of Azra. The chapter tightens the moral dilemma through conversations with his sister and the old-timer who knew his father. **Chapter 7 (≈1000 words):** Arthur makes his choice. He returns to the Antares cruiser (or arranges a second meeting) and presents a carefully reasoned alternative: a limited lease with oversight conditions that protect both his claim and Martian settlers. This triggers a tense negotiation and a subtle betrayal when one party attempts to force his hand through legal maneuvering. The chapter focuses on intellect and integrity prevailing over raw power. **Chapter 8 (≈1000 words):** Resolution and quiet coda. The negotiated settlement is signed; Arthur retains partial control and establishes a modest trust for indentured families. Hints are given of larger ripples—new treaties being drafted, the arms race slowed by a few years, Earth analysts recalculating their models. The story closes on Arthur back at his dome, watching the familiar Martian sunset, knowing he has altered the course of events far beyond his small plot while still remaining an ordinary engineer. The final tone is one of rational hope tempered by the knowledge that the larger game continues.
With a view to making the writing more human, discuss how a human might approach this particular piece (given the original prompt). Discuss telltale LLM approaches to writing (generally) and ways they might not serve this particular piece. For example, common LLM failings are to write safely, or to always wrap things up with a bow, or trying to write impressively at the expense of readability. Then do a deep dive on the intention & plan, critiquing ways it might be falling into typical LLM tropes & pitfalls. Brainstorm ideas to make it more human. Be comprehensive. We aren't doing any rewriting of the plan yet, just critique & brainstorming.
Model Output:
# Human Approach Discussion A human writer approaching this prompt—especially one aiming for an Asimov pastiche—would likely begin with a mix of genuine affection for the source material and personal idiosyncrasies that no prompt can fully capture. They might spend time rereading foundation-era Asimov stories not just for style but to internalize the particular flavor of restrained wonder, the way Asimov let ideas breathe through long conversations rather than action. The human would probably feel the tension between wanting to honor the “polite but forceful” opening and the explicit instruction to start Chapter 1 aboard the ship; they might jot down several different flashback lengths before settling on one that feels right intuitively rather than structurally perfect. A human would also bring autobiographical or emotional texture that isn’t in the prompt: perhaps a memory of working a summer job under a domineering boss and channeling that into Arthur’s stubbornness, or a real-world frustration with corporate lease agreements mapped onto the Martian mining system. They would likely oscillate between excitement at the big-picture world-building and anxiety that it might become preachy. The dialogue scene in Chapters 3–4 would be written in multiple passes—first getting the ideas down, then paring back anything that feels too on-the-nose, then reading it aloud to check rhythm. Pacing decisions would be more instinctive: a human might sense that 1000 words per chapter feels a little rigid and mentally reserve the right to let one chapter run long or short depending on how the scene breathes. Finally, a human would probably worry about the ending—wanting it to feel earned and quietly hopeful without sounding trite—and might scribble alternate final paragraphs in a notebook before committing. # Telltale LLM Approaches & Their Misfit Here LLMs commonly exhibit several patterns that can undermine this particular piece: - **Overly neat architecture**: Every chapter exactly ~1000 words, perfectly balanced, each fulfilling one clear function (introspection → arrival → dialogue → escalation → resolution). This produces competent but mechanical progression that lacks the slight unevenness of human storytelling, where some scenes linger because the writer fell in love with a conversation. - **Safe, bloodless politeness**: Because the prompt specifies “politely but forcefully,” an LLM will often make every interaction excessively courteous, draining genuine tension. The “diplomatic in name only” aspect can be told rather than felt if the prose stays too even-tempered. - **Impressive-but-hollow exposition**: LLM writing frequently inserts elegant world-building sentences that sound profound but don’t arise naturally from a character’s limited perspective. For an Asimov pastiche this is especially dangerous because Asimov’s exposition works precisely because it feels like a logical person thinking through implications, not because the narrator is showing off. - **Resolution tidiness**: LLMs love “wrapping with a bow”—giving every subplot a clean payoff and ending on a note of thematic closure. Asimov often preferred quiet, slightly ambiguous codas that suggested the larger historical currents would continue regardless of one man’s choice. A too-neat ending would betray the retro-classic tone. - **Uniform emotional temperature**: LLM prose tends to maintain the same thoughtful, slightly detached tone throughout. A human would allow micro-variations—Arthur’s irritation spiking when his sister is threatened, a moment of dry humor, a brief flash of genuine fear—creating texture. - **Vocabulary recycling**: LLMs often circle the same descriptive words (“austere,” “impeccably,” “veiled,” “subtly forceful”) because those words appeared in the prompt or earlier parts of the plan. This creates a telltale sameness that hurts readability. - **Plot symmetry**: The plan’s clean 4-chapter setup / 4-chapter consequences structure is classic LLM thinking—balanced and logical, but not necessarily the way a human’s mind wanders when imagining how a small miner would realistically experience being sucked into interstellar politics. # Deep Critique of the Current Intention & Chapter Plan The current plan is competent and clearly derived from the brainstorming, but it displays several LLM-typical weaknesses: - The 8 × 1000-word structure is too rigidly symmetrical. Chapter 3 and 4 both being “the meeting” feels like an artificial way to hit a dialogue quota rather than an organic decision about how long the scene needs to breathe. A human might have let the meeting run across 2.5 chapters or absorbed some of it into Chapter 2. - The character of Dr. Elias Korr is exactly the sort of smooth, high-ranking diplomat an LLM would generate—intelligent, courteous, vaguely menacing—without any memorable quirk or contradiction that would make him feel like a specific person. - Personal stakes (the sister) are introduced late (Chapter 6) in a way that feels checklist-driven rather than lived. A human writer who had decided the sister mattered would probably have seeded her existence much earlier, perhaps in Arthur’s thoughts while aboard the ship. - The “quiet coda” in Chapter 8 risks becoming the classic LLM “and then he looked at the sunset and reflected on how small he was yet how much he had changed” ending. The language in the plan (“rational hope tempered by the knowledge that the larger game continues”) is already sliding into generic philosophizing. - World-building delivery is still too evenly distributed. The plan says “small, organic doses” but the chapter summaries read like deliberate insertion points rather than moments when a character would naturally think about these things. - Tone description emphasizes “calm, rational, slightly dry” so relentlessly that it may produce prose that feels emotionally flat, missing the subtle undercurrent of mid-century sci-fi optimism mixed with unease that Asimov often carried. # Brainstorming Ideas to Make It More Human - **Introduce productive messiness**: Allow one chapter to run mentally long—perhaps let the meeting scene sprawl into Chapter 5 if the conversation demands it, then compress the escalation phase. Consider starting Chapter 1 not with perfectly controlled introspection but with Arthur noticing a small physical discomfort (the recycled air giving him a headache, the chair’s outdated padding) that leads into memory. - **Give characters contradictions and small tics**: Let Arthur be more irritable than purely stoic—he might swear under his breath at the diplomatic courtesy while still being intelligent. Make Lieutenant Voss occasionally show a flicker of genuine belief in Antares’ mission rather than remaining a perfect functionary. Give Korr one humanizing detail (perhaps he genuinely loves Martian geology and keeps drifting into enthusiastic technical talk before catching himself). - **Seed personal elements earlier and unevenly**: Mention the sister in Chapter 1 as a passing worry (“he hoped the company wasn’t leaning on her again”) so her appearance in Chapter 6 feels like payoff rather than introduction. Let Arthur’s old-timer friend appear as an unexpected voice on a comm call in Chapter 5 instead of waiting for a dedicated scene. - **Vary sentence rhythm and vocabulary**: Deliberately plan to use shorter, blunter sentences during moments of frustration or realization. Avoid repeating prompt-derived words like “politely but forcefully,” “veiled,” “subtly.” Let some descriptions be slightly inefficient or idiosyncratic—perhaps Arthur notices the absurdly large brass fittings on the cruiser’s airlock because they remind him of his grandfather’s plumbing. - **Allow moral ambiguity in the ending**: Brainstorm several possible codas where Arthur’s compromise is not entirely heroic. Maybe the limited lease he negotiates still displaces a dozen indentured families elsewhere. Or perhaps he realizes he has only delayed the arms race, not stopped it, and the story ends on a quiet note of doubt rather than tempered hope. - **Inject small human physicality and domesticity**: Throughout the plan, add moments where Arthur thinks about unfinished practical tasks back home (a balky water pump, a half-planted hydroponic tray, the way the dust gets into everything). These details ground the grand politics in a way that feels lived rather than conceptual. - **Embrace micro-variations in tone**: Allow a scene of dry, understated humor—perhaps Arthur mentally comparing the diplomatic meeting to arguing with the mining regulatory board over lease fees. Let one moment of real fear or anger break through the rational surface, then have Arthur consciously tamp it down because that’s how he copes. - **Loosen structural perfection**: Consider whether the Antares meeting needs to be the only major set piece. Maybe a second, smaller confrontation on Mars in Chapter 7 could be more revealing than returning to the cruiser. Or let Chapter 8 be shorter and more elliptical, ending on an image rather than a summary of consequences. - **Write some sections “wrong” on purpose**: A human might draft the meeting dialogue with too much exposition first, then cut it brutally in revision. The plan could be improved by imagining which sections the writer would struggle with most (probably sustaining polite menace across two full chapters) and planning extra attention there. - **Channel specific Asimov stories**: A human would likely keep mental touchstones—“The Martian Way” for the settler’s pride, “The Big and the Little” for the scale contrast, “The Feeling of Power” for the idea that simple human stubbornness can still matter. These influences would create small stylistic echoes that aren’t in the current plan. These critiques and ideas remain at the level of analysis; no actual rewriting of the plan has been done. The goal is to push the piece away from polished, symmetrical, safely rational LLM output and toward something that feels like it came from a specific writer who loves classic sci-fi but also brings their own small obsessions, irritations, and human unevenness to the keyboard.
Ok now with these considerations in mind, formulate the final plan for the a humanlike, compelling short piece in {n_chapters} chapters. Bear in mind the constraints of the piece (each chapter is just 1000 words). Above all things, the plan must serve the original prompt. We will use the same format as before: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
**# Intention** The piece will be written in a calm but not bloodless third-person voice that channels classic Asimov: precise, idea-driven, with world-building emerging naturally through a practical man’s thoughts and conversations rather than elegant narration. The tone aims for retrofuturistic restraint—bulky atomic rockets, chrome-trimmed domes, slide-rule assisted calculations alongside early positronic notaries—while allowing small, human textures: physical discomfort, domestic worries, moments of dry irritation, and flashes of genuine unease beneath the rational surface. Big-picture stakes (corporate gold rush, interstellar arms race, Earth’s demographic decline, the 2147 treaties) will appear in uneven, character-grounded doses rather than evenly distributed exposition. Arthur Neegan is portrayed as a stubborn, competent, first-generation settler who is neither saint nor genius; he is pragmatic, occasionally irritable, and worried about unfinished chores on his claim. The central conflict remains a political-economic-moral tug-of-war without mystical elements. His personal stake—his younger sister Lena working as an indentured electronics tech on a neighboring lease—is seeded early and unevenly so it feels lived rather than introduced by checklist. The pivotal meeting with the Antares diplomat (Dr. Elias Korr) is kept dialogue-heavy across two chapters but allowed to breathe with slight unevenness in pacing; Korr is given one or two specific quirks (a genuine enthusiasm for geology that leaks through his diplomatic mask) to avoid generic smoothness. Stylistic choices: sentence rhythm will vary—shorter, blunter sentences during moments of frustration or realization; occasional inefficient, character-specific observations (the way dust settles, the absurd brass fittings on airlocks) to prevent polished sameness. Vocabulary will be deliberately non-repetitive, avoiding over-reliance on prompt words like “politely,” “veiled,” or “subtly.” The story begins exactly as required: Chapter 1 opens aboard the transfer vessel with Arthur wondering what he has become involved in, using brief, natural flashbacks to the armed retinue’s arrival that morning. The piece avoids tidy bows, overly symmetrical chapter functions, and purely rational hope; the ending will be quiet, slightly ambiguous, and focused on one ordinary engineer’s small victory within a still-dangerous larger game. Chapters remain capped near 1000 words, but the plan allows modest flexibility in emphasis so that the long dialogue scene does not feel artificially split and the consequences on Mars feel grounded and human. Above all, the plan strictly serves the original prompt while incorporating human messiness, emotional micro-variations, and readable clarity over impressive phrasing. **# Chapter Planning** **Chapter 1 (≈1000 words):** Arthur sits in a worn passenger seat aboard the Antares transfer vessel, nursing a low-grade headache from the recycled air and wondering how his quiet life turned into this. Through brief, irritated flashbacks he recalls the armed retinue—badges bearing the Antares diplomatic crest—landing at his dome that morning, their leader Lieutenant Mara Voss delivering a courteous but non-negotiable invitation. He thinks about discovering the rich Azra vein, the sudden swarm of survey drones, and his unfinished irrigation lines back home. First scattered hints of the corporate lease system and distant arms race appear through his practical worries. The chapter ends as the vessel begins docking maneuvers with the larger cruiser. **Chapter 2 (≈1000 words):** Escorted through the cruiser’s retrofuturistic corridors by Voss and two silent guards, Arthur notices small human details: the oversized brass airlock fittings that remind him of his grandfather’s plumbing, the faint smell of ozone. Voss remains impeccably professional but lets one dry remark slip that suggests she has done this escort duty too many times. Arthur is deposited in a waiting lounge where minor functionaries drop oblique references to rival Earth corporations and Jovian interests. Tension builds quietly through his growing impatience. The chapter closes as he is finally shown into the formal meeting room. **Chapter 3 (≈1000 words):** Arthur meets Dr. Elias Korr, a senior Antares diplomat who turns out to be more than a smooth operator: Korr’s eyes light up when conversation drifts toward the actual mineralogy of Azra, betraying a geologist’s passion beneath the diplomatic veneer. The scene is primarily dialogue. Korr makes a carefully phrased offer of protection and generous compensation for exclusive rights, framing it as necessary for solar system stability. Arthur pushes back with blunt engineering questions and refuses to sign immediately. Through their exchange the reader learns the stakes—Azra’s role in both clean drives and horrific weapons, the current arms race between Earth, the Belt, and the colonies—without feeling lectured. The chapter ends mid-conversation on Arthur’s cautious counter-question about what happens to workers like his sister Lena. **Chapter 4 (≈1000 words):** The meeting continues, now with Korr’s mask slipping slightly when Arthur mentions Lena. Korr becomes firmer, alluding to the precarious legal position of indentured families and the real possibility of “reallocation” if Arthur proves uncooperative. Arthur holds his ground using plain-spoken arguments rooted in his settler experience. Further world-building emerges organically: references to the 2147 treaties, the gold-rush chaos on Mars, and Earth’s quiet desperation for new energy sources. The scene ends with Korr presenting a formal contract and granting Arthur 48 hours to decide. Arthur is returned to the transfer vessel, unsettled and already turning the problem over in his mind. **Chapter 5 (≈1000 words):** Back under Mars’ dusty sky, Arthur finds his claim already crawling with rival probes and an injunction notice pinned to his airlock. He shares a pot of bitter synthetic coffee with his taciturn foreman, who is clearly frightened. A short, awkward comm call with Lena reveals she is being watched. Arthur begins quiet, practical investigation—checking old survey logs, talking to an old-timer who knew his father—while worrying about the half-repaired water pump still leaking in the greenhouse. The chapter focuses on his rising irritation and the feeling of being squeezed from all sides. **Chapter 6 (≈1000 words):** Pressure escalates when a Helios Combine representative makes a cruder, more tempting counter-offer in person, promising immediate relief for Lena if Arthur signs with them instead. Arthur visits his sister’s lease under the pretense of a family visit and sees firsthand the fear in the indentured crews. A tense conversation with Lena forces him to confront the personal cost of refusal. The chapter mixes small domestic details (the familiar smell of her dome’s air, the half-finished model rocket she’s kept since childhood) with the growing realization that no choice is clean. **Chapter 7 (≈1000 words):** Arthur returns to the Antares cruiser for a second, shorter meeting. He presents a carefully reasoned compromise: a limited lease with independent Martian oversight and protections written in for indentured families. Korr listens, Voss shows a flicker of unexpected respect, but a last-minute legal maneuver by Helios attempts to invalidate Arthur’s counter-offer. The chapter is dialogue- and intellect-driven, showing Arthur’s stubborn integrity clashing with corporate machinery. He ultimately forces a provisional agreement, knowing it is imperfect. **Chapter 8 (≈1000 words):** The settlement is signed under tight deadlines. Arthur retains nominal ownership of part of the vein and establishes a modest trust for affected families, though he is quietly aware that larger powers have only been slowed, not stopped. He returns to his dome, fixes the leaking water pump as the Martian sunset bleeds across the regolith, and shares a final comm with Lena. The story closes on a quiet, slightly uncertain note: one ordinary engineer has nudged history, but the arms race and the mining leases will continue. No grand summation, only the small satisfaction of having refused to be merely a pawn.
Perfect. Now with the outline more crystallised, and bearing in mind the discussion on human writing vs LLM pitfalls, we will flesh out our characters. Lets go through each of our main characters: - Write about their background, personality, idiosyncracies, flaws. Be specific and come up with examples to anchor & ground the character's profile (both core and trivial) - Briefly describe their physicality: appearance, how they carry themselves, express, interact with the world. - Concisely detail their motives, allegiences and existing relationships. Think from the perspective of the character as a real breathing thinking feeling individual in this world. - Write a couple quotes of flavour dialogue / internal monologue from the character to experiment with their voice. Output like this: # Character 1 name <character exploration> # Character 2 name <character exploration> etc
Model Output:
# Arthur Neegan **Background, personality, idiosyncrasies, flaws:** Arthur is a 44-year-old first-generation Martian, born in a prefab dome during the second wave of settlement when Mars still felt like a frontier rather than a corporate leasehold. He trained himself in geology and mining engineering by poring over old Earth textbooks and apprenticing under an ex-terrestrial roughneck who taught him that “the rock doesn’t lie, but the contracts will.” His personality is pragmatic, stubborn, and quietly moral, but he has a short fuse for bureaucratic nonsense. Idiosyncrasy: he still keeps a battered slide-rule in his pocket even though the newer positronic calculators are faster; he claims it helps him “feel the numbers.” Flaw: he tends to bottle up worry until it leaks out as sarcasm or silence, and he can be slow to trust outsiders because he has watched too many friends lose their claims to “reallocation clauses.” Example: he once spent three weeks arguing with a corporate inspector over a two-percent variance in water usage rather than simply paying the fine, because “principle matters in the small print too.” **Physicality:** Medium height, broad-shouldered from years in low-g labor, with a slight permanent stoop from crawling through access tunnels. Weathered face, dust lines permanently etched around his eyes, short-cropped brown hair going grey at the temples. He moves with economical care, never wasting motion, the way men do who have repaired machinery in bulky pressure suits. When agitated he rubs the callused pad of his left thumb against his slide-rule case. **Motives, allegiances, relationships:** Arthur’s core motive is to keep his modest claim intact so he can leave something real to the next generation of Martians instead of another corporate lease. He feels a weary loyalty to the idea of Martian self-sufficiency but has no love for revolutionary rhetoric. His most important relationship is with his younger sister Lena (38), who was indentured after their father’s death to pay off family debts; he feels quietly guilty that he kept his own lease while she did not. He maintains a gruff but genuine friendship with his foreman, Tomas, and a wary respect for the old-timer prospector who taught him the trade. He views the great powers—Antares, Helios, Earth—as equally dangerous landlords. **Voice examples:** Internal monologue: “They can dress it up in diplomatic ribbon and call it interstellar stability, but it still smells like another man trying to tell me what my own rock is worth.” Spoken: “Doctor, I’ve spent twenty years listening to the ground. It doesn’t much care about your treaties. So maybe we talk about what it actually needs before we start signing pieces of my life away.” # Lieutenant Mara Voss **Background, personality, idiosyncrasies, flaws:** Mara Voss, 37, rose through the Antares security services after a stint in the Jovian border patrols. She is “diplomatic security” in the way that certain agencies are diplomatic in name only—polite, precise, and utterly unyielding. Personality: professional, dryly witty, but carries an underlying belief that order is worth small moral compromises. Idiosyncrasy: she unconsciously aligns every object on a table before speaking, a leftover habit from years of military inspections. Flaw: she sometimes convinces herself that following orders is the same as doing the right thing, which lets her justify pressure on people like Arthur. Example: she once escorted a protesting colonist off an asteroid habitat while quoting treaty clauses in a calm voice even as the man was in tears. **Physicality:** Tall and straight-backed, moves with parade-ground economy. Sharp features, dark hair pulled into a severe knot, uniform always immaculate. Her expression is politely neutral until she is genuinely amused—then one corner of her mouth twitches upward in a half-smile that never quite becomes a grin. She keeps her hands clasped behind her back when listening, a posture that makes her seem both attentive and unreachable. **Motives, allegiances, relationships:** Voss believes Antares represents the only realistic chance of preventing another resource war; she is loyal to the service rather than any single superior. She feels a detached professional sympathy for Arthur but will not hesitate to lean on him if ordered. Her only real personal tie in the story is a distant, slightly strained respect for Dr. Korr, whose intelligence she admires even when she disagrees with his methods. She has no close family left on Earth. **Voice examples:** Spoken: “Mr. Neegan, I have delivered twenty-seven such invitations. All of them were accepted. The ones that weren’t tended to regret the decision later. I’d rather not add you to that list.” Internal monologue: “He’s stubborn, not stupid. That makes him harder to move, but easier to predict. Just keep the tone polite; the law does the rest.” # Dr. Elias Korr **Background, personality, idiosyncrasies, flaws:** Elias Korr, 58, began his career as a geologist for the old Earth Fusion Authority before transitioning into Antares diplomatic service when the funding dried up. He is a true believer in managed resource allocation but still gets boyishly excited by unusual mineral specimens. Personality: urbane, patient, intellectually arrogant beneath the charm. Idiosyncrasy: when genuinely interested he leans forward and taps one finger on the table in a precise rhythm, almost like he is conducting an invisible orchestra of data. Flaw: he tends to view individuals as data points in a larger stability equation, which lets him justify coercion without feeling like a villain. Example: he once delayed a family’s resettlement permit for six months because moving them would have disrupted a projected mining yield by 0.8 percent. **Physicality:** Slightly overweight in the soft way of men who now spend more time in meetings than in the field. Neat silver beard, wire-rimmed spectacles that look faintly old-fashioned even for this retrofuturistic era. He carries himself with relaxed authority, hands usually steepled or lightly touching whatever object is nearest, as if grounding himself in the physical world. **Motives, allegiances, relationships:** Korr’s deepest motive is to prevent Azra from triggering an uncontrollable arms race that could devastate the inner system; he genuinely believes centralized Antares control is the least-bad option. He is loyal to the Antares Diplomatic Corps but also to his own vision of “rational stewardship.” He has a cordial but distant relationship with Voss (she executes, he decides) and no personal connection to Arthur before this meeting. He is quietly estranged from a grown daughter on Earth. **Voice examples:** Spoken: “The rock beneath your boots, Mr. Neegan, is no longer simply rock. It is leverage. And leverage, like any force, must be applied with care or the entire structure collapses.” Internal monologue: “He actually cares about the water pump more than the strategic balance. Remarkable. Annoying, but remarkable. Perhaps that narrow focus is exactly why he matters.” # Lena Neegan **Background, personality, idiosyncrasies, flaws:** Lena, 38, is Arthur’s younger sister. She was indentured at 22 after their father defaulted on a drilling loan. Trained as an electronics and drive-systems tech, she now maintains Azra survey equipment for Helios Combine under a long-term contract. Personality: practical like her brother but more openly cynical and quicker to anger. Idiosyncrasy: she keeps a half-finished plastic model rocket from childhood on her workbench and fiddles with it when stressed. Flaw: she sometimes lashes out at Arthur for “still playing homesteader while the rest of us live in the real world,” even though she knows he tried to buy out her indenture. **Physicality:** Shorter than Arthur, wiry, with the same dust-lined eyes but sharper features. Hair usually tied back with whatever spare wire is handy. She moves quickly and impatiently, gestures with her hands when talking, and has a habit of wiping her palms on her coveralls even when they’re clean. **Motives, allegiances, relationships:** Lena wants her contract ended and enough savings to start her own small repair shop. She is loyal first to her own survival, second to her brother (though she resents needing him), and has no love for any of the corporations. Her relationship with Arthur is loving but prickly; she worries he is going to get himself killed or ruined trying to protect an idea of independence that no longer exists. **Voice examples:** Spoken: “Don’t go playing hero on my account, Art. I’ve been breathing corporate air longer than you’ve been fighting it. Just make the deal that gets me out of this damn suit.” Internal monologue: “He still thinks he can bargain with them like it’s a water-rights dispute back home. God, I love him and I want to shake him at the same time.”

Chapter 1

Great. Now let's write this. Follow your plan. Output chapter 1, without any additional commentary. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Arthur Neegan sat in the scuffed acceleration couch of the Antares transfer vessel and tried not to think about the leaking irrigation pump back at the dome. The headache was already starting, the familiar low throb that came from breathing air that had passed through too many other lungs. He rubbed the callused pad of his left thumb against the worn case of his slide-rule, the small repetitive motion steadying him the way it always had. Around him the cabin hummed with the quiet, businesslike vibration of atomic thrusters. No windows.
